def_en: a metal fastening that confines the wrists or ankles; something that restricts freedom
def_zh: iron shackles, shackle of tradition, break the shackles
quote_en: The prisoner's shackles clanked against the floor as he shuffled toward the judge.
quote_zh: Ajarry felt the cold weight of the shackle on her ankle as she was marched from the dungeon to the ship, one link connecting her to a chain of human misery.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "shackle" both literally and metaphorically: the iron on Cora's wrists is inseparable from the psychological shackles of fear and inherited trauma that bind generations.
ex_zh: By opening the novel with Ajarry in shackles and ending with Cora's uncertain freedom, Whitehead frames the entire narrative as a question: can a body ever be truly free when the mind still wears the shackles of the past?

def_en: to obstruct or hinder the progress of something
def_zh: stymie progress, stymie an attempt, stymie a plan
quote_en: The detective's investigation was stymied by a complete lack of physical evidence.
quote_zh: The patrollers stymied countless escape attempts, yet each thwarted flight only deepened the fugitives' resolve to find another way north.
ex_en: Whitehead suggests that the slave-catching system, for all its brutality, cannot ultimately stymie the human desire for freedom—every blocked route creates new ingenuity.
ex_zh: The novel's structure, with each state presenting a new obstacle designed to stymie Cora's progress, mirrors the historical reality that the underground railroad succeeded precisely because it could not be permanently stymied by any single law or posse.

def_en: an item of personal property; historically, a slave considered as property
def_zh: chattel slavery, human chattel, as mere chattel
quote_en: In the eyes of the law at that time, enslaved people were treated as chattel rather than human beings.
quote_zh: Ajarry understood the brutal arithmetic of the plantation: a slave was chattel, her value fluctuating like cotton prices, her humanity irrelevant to the balance sheet.
ex_en: By persistently using "chattel" to describe enslaved people, Whitehead exposes the dehumanizing logic of the plantation economy, where human worth is reduced to a price on a bill of sale.
ex_zh: The novel's use of legal and economic vocabulary—"chattel," "liquidate," "property"—creates an ironic contrast with the Declaration of Independence's claim that "all men are created equal," forcing readers to confront the gap between American ideals and practice.

def_en: the state of being enslaved or physically restrained
def_zh: in bondage, escape from bondage, spiritual bondage
quote_en: The novel explores the psychological bondage that persists long after physical chains are removed.
quote_zh: Mabel's brief taste of freedom in the swamp proved that bondage was not merely an absence of chains but the systematic destruction of the will to escape.
ex_en: Whitehead distinguishes between physical bondage and the internalized submission that keeps the enslaved compliant even when no overseer is watching—the true horror of the system.
ex_zh: The underground railroad represents a literal escape from bondage, but the novel repeatedly asks whether any character, even those who reach the North, ever fully escapes the psychic bondage of having been owned.

def_en: a person who stares openly in a foolish or rude manner
def_zh: idle gawkers, a crowd of gawkers, gawkers and bystanders
quote_en: The accident drew a crowd of gawkers who did nothing to help.
quote_zh: The auction block attracted gawkers who chewed oysters and corn while human beings were stripped naked and appraised like livestock.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gawker" to indict the ordinary white citizens whose passive complicity—watching, consuming, doing nothing—enables the slave system to function.
ex_zh: The gawkers at the auction foreshadow the Friday night crowds in North Carolina who watch lynchings as entertainment, revealing that spectatorship is itself a form of violence.

def_en: a chain used to restrain a prisoner; to confine or restrict
def_zh: fetter with chains, break the fetters, fetters of convention
quote_en: The outdated traditions fettered the company's ability to innovate.
quote_zh: Each fetter on Cora's wrists was a reminder that her body was not her own, yet her mind remained the one territory no master could chain.
ex_en: Whitehead transforms the literal fetter into a metaphor for the limitations that race, gender, and class impose on human possibility in nineteenth-century America.
ex_zh: The novel's recurring image of fetters—on Ajarry, on Cora, on the captives in the slave ship exhibit—creates a visual leitmotif that ties individual suffering to the larger machinery of American bondage.

def_en: to convert assets into cash; to eliminate or kill
def_zh: liquidate assets, liquidate a debt, liquidate opponents
quote_en: The company was forced to liquidate its holdings after declaring bankruptcy.
quote_zh: When a master's debts mounted, he would liquidate his human property without hesitation, scattering families across states as casually as discarding worn tools.
ex_en: Whitehead's use of the cold commercial verb "liquidate" to describe the sale of human beings reveals the economic rationalism that underlies chattel slavery: people are assets to be cashed out.
ex_zh: The novel forces readers to recognize that the language of modern finance—liquidation, assets, property—was perfected on the bodies of the enslaved, making American capitalism inseparable from American slavery.

def_en: a large estate farmed by resident laborers, historically enslaved people
def_zh: cotton plantation, plantation owner, plantation economy
quote_en: The family had owned the plantation for five generations before the war.
quote_zh: The Randall plantation was not a home but a factory of suffering, its soil fertilized by sweat and blood, its harvest measured in bales of cotton and broken bodies.
ex_en: Whitehead reimagines the plantation not as the pastoral setting of lost-cause mythology but as a total institution designed to extract maximum labor while systematically destroying human identity.
ex_zh: Each section of the novel presents a different model of racial control, but the plantation remains the ur-form—the original machine whose logic of extraction and dehumanization spreads to every state Cora visits.

def_en: easily bent or influenced; flexible
def_zh: pliable material, pliable personality, pliable and obedient
quote_en: The young politician proved pliable under pressure from party leaders.
quote_zh: Slave traders prized pliable disposition above all else, for a compliant slave required less breaking and yielded more years of labor before death or despair took hold.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "pliable" ironically: the masters' desire for pliable slaves is exactly what creates the conditions for rebellion, because those who break too easily also break inside.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that Cora survives not because she is pliable but because she is stubbornly resistant—her refusal to be shaped by the plantation is precisely what makes her a fugitive.

def_en: to be in agreement; to make mocking remarks
def_zh: jibe with the facts, jibe at someone's expense, make a jibe
quote_en: His account of the incident did not jibe with what the other witnesses reported.
quote_zh: The slave owners' claims of benevolent mastery did not jibe with the scars on every back in the quarters, nor with the screams that pierced each night.
ex_en: Whitehead's narrative exposes the fundamental contradiction between the ideology of paternalistic slavery and its brutal reality, showing how the two cannot jibe.
ex_zh: The novel repeatedly stages moments where official stories—the happy plantation, the civilizing mission—fail to jibe with lived experience, creating a gap that fugitives like Cora slip through.

def_en: extremely bad, severe, or degrading; showing utter hopelessness
def_zh: abject poverty, abject misery, abject apology
quote_en: The refugees lived in abject poverty, without clean water or shelter.
quote_zh: The Hob women inhabited a state of abject misery that the plantation had engineered—too broken for the fields, too wretched to be anything but a warning.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "abject" to describe a condition that is not merely suffering but the complete erasure of dignity and agency, a state of being reduced to pure victimhood.
ex_zh: The Hob women function as a cautionary exhibit within the novel, showing what happens when the system succeeds in making a person abject—yet Cora's survival proves that even abjection can be a form of camouflage.

def_en: to behave or conduct oneself in a particular manner
def_zh: comport oneself with dignity, comport with standards, well-comported
quote_en: The professor comported himself with dignity even under hostile questioning.
quote_zh: Old Abraham comported himself with the weary dignity of a man sold too many times, his cynicism a shield against the hope that had only ever brought him pain.
ex_en: Whitehead shows that how a character comports themselves on the plantation is a survival strategy, not a reflection of innate character—the mask is not the face.
ex_zh: The contrast between how Cora comports herself in Georgia (silent, watchful) and in South Carolina (learning, adapting) reveals that comportment is situational, a performance demanded by power rather than an expression of self.

def_en: a person who dislikes or distrusts humankind
def_zh: bitter misanthrope, cynical misanthrope, misanthrope recluse
quote_en: The old man lived as a recluse, his reputation as a misanthrope well earned.
quote_zh: Ridgeway was no mere misanthrope; his hatred was exquisitely targeted at those who dared to believe their bondage could be escaped, at the very notion of hope itself.
ex_en: Whitehead complicates the figure of the misanthrope by making Ridgeway a man who hates not all humanity but specifically the part of humanity that refuses to accept its assigned subjugation.
ex_zh: Ridgeway's misanthropy is the logical endpoint of the slave catcher's worldview: if you believe some people are property, then anyone who claims to be a person is a threat to the order you serve.

def_en: feeling or revealing crude sexual desire
def_zh: lascivious glance, lascivious behavior, lascivious intent
quote_en: The manager's lascivious comments created a hostile work environment.
quote_zh: Terrance Randall's lascivious gaze swept over the enslaved women as casually as a farmer appraising livestock, his power to take whatever he desired the ultimate expression of bondage.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lascivious" to show how sexual predation is not a deviation from slavery but one of its core features—the master's right to the bodies of the enslaved.
ex_zh: The novel refuses to sensationalize sexual violence, but the repeated presence of lascivious white men (Terrance, the slave traders, the night riders) establishes that the female slave's body is never safe, even in supposed refuges like South Carolina's dormitories.

def_en: to make a liquid turbid or muddy; to disturb or agitate
def_zh: roil the waters, roil emotions, roil the public
quote_en: Political scandals continued to roil the nation throughout the summer.
quote_zh: The underground railroad roiled the southern states like a sickness in the blood, each fugitive a symptom of the deeper disease that slavery had inflicted upon the American soul.
ex_en: Whitehead uses the visceral verb "roil" to describe the effect of fugitives on the social order—they disturb the settled sediment of racial hierarchy, making clear what was previously hidden.
ex_zh: The novel's structure, which moves Cora through different states with different systems of racial control, shows how each new environment is roiled by her very presence, as if a single fugitive carries enough disruption to unsettle an entire society.

def_en: the responsible management of something entrusted to one's care
def_zh: environmental stewardship, financial stewardship, stewardship of resources
quote_en: The tribe has maintained stewardship of these lands for over a thousand years.
quote_zh: James Randall's stewardship of the northern half was brutal but predictable; his brother Terrance's reign replaced order with caprice, proving that even the least terrible master was still a master.
ex_en: Whitehead ironizes the word "stewardship" by applying it to slaveholders—they claim to be responsible stewards of their human property, but the novel shows this as self-serving hypocrisy.
ex_zh: The contrast between James's calculated stewardship and Terrance's chaotic cruelty reveals that there is no good stewardship of slavery, only degrees of evil; the very concept is a contradiction in terms.

def_en: nervous or easily frightened; fickle and unpredictable
def_zh: skittish horse, skittish investor, skittish behavior
quote_en: Investors remained skittish after the market's sudden collapse.
quote_zh: The slaves became skittish whenever the Randall brothers appeared together, for history had taught them that such reunions always preceded some new cruelty they could not yet imagine.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "skittish" to describe the hypervigilance of the enslaved, a constant state of alertness that is itself a form of psychological torture.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that skittishness is not a character flaw but a rational response to an environment where danger is unpredictable and can come from any direction at any time—the slaves are not paranoid; the system really is out to get them.

def_en: a sudden and unaccountable change of mood or behavior
def_zh: mere caprice, caprice of fortune, caprice of a tyrant
quote_en: The dictator ruled by caprice, executing loyal servants one day and promoting traitors the next.
quote_zh: On the plantation, survival depended on predicting the master's caprice, but Terrance Randall's whims were as unpredictable as lightning—and just as deadly to those in his path.
ex_en: Whitehead shows that the master's caprice is not a departure from the logic of slavery but its essence: the absolute power to act without reason or accountability.
ex_zh: The novel repeatedly contrasts the caprice of individual masters with the systematic rationality of the plantation economy, revealing a fundamental instability: the system depends on arbitrary violence, but arbitrary violence also threatens to destroy the system's productivity.

def_en: a state of dreamy meditation or fanciful thinking
def_zh: lost in reverie, brief reverie, romantic reverie
quote_en: She was lost in reverie, imagining a life far from the factory walls.
quote_zh: For a brief reverie on Jockey's birthday, the slaves danced as if they were free, but the appearance of the masters shattered the illusion and returned them to the brutal arithmetic of the cotton rows.
ex_en: Whitehead treats reverie as both a necessary psychological escape and a dangerous distraction—moments of imagined freedom make bondage bearable but also make its return more crushing.
ex_zh: The novel's structure, which moves from Georgia's plantation to South Carolina's false utopia to North Carolina's terror, suggests that every reverie of freedom in the South is a trap waiting to spring.

def_en: a person's face or facial expression; to tolerate or approve
def_zh: stern countenance, friendly countenance, countenance bad behavior
quote_en: The judge refused to countenance any further delays in the proceedings.
quote_zh: James Randall's stoic countenance betrayed nothing as Terrance ordered the whippings, but the slaves had learned to read the small signs—the tightened jaw, the averted eyes—that revealed his silent complicity.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "countenance" as both noun and verb to explore the theme of silent complicity: the face that shows nothing is still countenancing evil.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that to countenance (tolerate) slavery is to have a countenance (face) that reveals nothing—the blank expression of the bystander who does nothing is itself a moral choice.

def_en: an unprincipled or wicked person; morally depraved
def_zh: old reprobate, reprobate behavior, reprobate and scoundrel
quote_en: The old reprobate had outlived all his friends and most of his enemies.
quote_zh: The tutor who taught Michael the Declaration of Independence was a reprobate chased from every decent position, yet his revenge was to plant the seeds of liberty in a slave's mind.
ex_en: Whitehead complicates the figure of the reprobate by suggesting that moral depravity can sometimes produce unintended good—the corrupt tutor becomes an accidental liberator.
ex_zh: The novel refuses simple moral categories: a reprobate can be the vehicle for enlightenment, while upstanding citizens (like the white townspeople of North Carolina) participate in lynchings, forcing readers to question what "reprobate" really means in a slave society.

def_en: to make confused or unable to think clearly
def_zh: addle the mind, addle one's wits, addle-brained
quote_en: Years of isolation had addled his mind to the point where he could no longer hold a conversation.
quote_zh: The constant terror of the plantation addled the senses of even the strongest slaves, until some could not distinguish between a genuine opportunity for escape and a trap laid by their masters.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "addle" to describe a specific form of psychological damage caused by slavery: not just suffering but the erosion of the ability to perceive reality accurately.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that the most insidious effect of slavery is that it addles the distinction between safety and danger, friend and enemy, hope and delusion—making the enslaved complicit in their own confinement.

def_en: to conceal one's true motives or feelings; to pretend
def_zh: dissemble one's intentions, dissemble ignorance, skill at dissembling
quote_en: The politician dissembled skillfully, never revealing his true position on the issue.
quote_zh: Crooked Ava had not lost her power to dissemble since her days of harassing Cora—she hooted and stomped as if it were the height of celebration, wearing the mask that survival demanded.
ex_en: Whitehead shows that dissembling is not a moral failing in the enslaved but a survival skill: the ability to hide one's true thoughts is the difference between living and dying.
ex_zh: The novel's many scenes of performance—slaves dancing for the masters, Cora pretending to be Bessie, the coon shows in North Carolina—all revolve around the art of dissembling, revealing that the entire social order is built on a shared fiction.

def_en: to skip or dance about in a lively manner; a playful leap
def_zh: caper about, caper with joy, criminal caper
quote_en: The children capered across the lawn, chasing fireflies into the dusk.
quote_zh: The slaves capered and hollered when the masters appeared, performing a grotesque spectacle of joy to conceal the terror that tightened their chests with every forced smile.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "caper" to expose the grotesque theatricality of plantation life, where every display of happiness is a coerced performance for the master's amusement.
ex_zh: The novel forces readers to see the capering slave as a tragic figure: the more joyfully he dances, the more complete his submission, turning the very expression of life into an advertisement for death.

def_en: to smile in a silly, self-conscious, or affected way
def_zh: simper at, simpering smile, simper with embarrassment
quote_en: The servant simpered at every word her master spoke, desperate to please.
quote_zh: Chester simpered and bowed before Terrance Randall, his desperate apologies doing nothing to stop the cane that was about to crash across his shoulders.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "simper" to capture the pathetic futility of appeasement—the simpering slave hopes that submission will deflect violence, but it never does.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of Chester's simper and the resulting beating demonstrates that for the slave, no amount of deference is enough; the master's violence is not a response to behavior but an assertion of power that seeks any excuse.

def_en: occurring at an inconvenient or inappropriate time
def_zh: inopportune moment, inopportune interruption, inopportune arrival
quote_en: His arrival was inopportune, interrupting a private conversation that could not be resumed.
quote_zh: The Randall brothers' appearance at Jockey's birthday was doubly inopportune—it shattered the slaves' brief illusion of freedom and introduced a cruelty that would forever mark the night.
ex_en: Whitehead shows that for the enslaved, there is no opportune time; any moment of apparent peace is always already threatened by the possibility of violence, making every celebration inopportune.
ex_zh: The novel's use of festivals and holidays (Jockey's birthday, the Friday night gatherings) as settings for violence suggests that the masters deliberately choose inopportune moments to maximize the psychological impact, turning joy into dread.

def_en: to groom or adorn oneself; to show self-satisfaction
def_zh: preen oneself, preen before a mirror, preen with pride
quote_en: The actor preened before the mirror, adjusting his collar for the fifth time.
quote_zh: Nag preened after Connelly bestowed his favors upon her, gloating over the ill treatment that she alone escaped—until the day her enemies had waited for finally arrived.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "preen" to expose the fragility of the slave's temporary advantage: those who preen over their favored status are setting themselves up for a fall, because favor can be withdrawn at any moment.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of Nag's preening and subsequent humiliation shows how the plantation system creates false hierarchies among the enslaved, turning them against each other to prevent solidarity.

def_en: a settling of accounts; a time of punishment or reward
def_zh: day of reckoning, final reckoning, moral reckoning
quote_en: The corrupt official knew that a reckoning was coming, but he continued his schemes anyway.
quote_zh: The Justice Convention in North Carolina represented a reckoning with the "colored question"—a decision to abolish not slavery but black people themselves, through expulsion and murder.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "reckoning" to describe moments when a society confronts its foundational contradictions, usually choosing violence over resolution.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that America has never truly had its reckoning with slavery; instead, each generation produces new forms of racial control (South Carolina's medical experiments, North Carolina's expulsions) that postpone the final account.

def_en: lacking a plan, purpose, or enthusiasm; disconnected
def_zh: desultory conversation, desultory effort, desultory reading
quote_en: The students made a desultory effort on their final project, clearly exhausted from the semester.
quote_zh: The northern and southern halves of the Randall plantation exchanged slaves in a desultory game, unloading beat niggers and skulky workers on each other as if human beings were worthless tokens.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "desultory" to capture the casual cruelty of the plantation system, where the movement of human beings is treated as an afterthought, a game played by masters bored with their power.
ex_zh: The novel's desultory structure—moving from one state to another without a clear linear progression—mirrors the randomness with which slaves were bought, sold, and traded, suggesting that there is no coherent narrative to be made of such suffering.

def_en: a group of people summoned by a sheriff to enforce the law; any armed group
def_zh: posse comitatus, posse of deputies, form a posse
quote_en: The sheriff assembled a posse to track the fugitives through the mountains.
quote_zh: Ridgeway rode with a posse of disreputable men, led by a fearsome Indian scout who wore a necklace of shriveled ears—each trophy a testament to a captured fugitive.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "posse" to demystify law enforcement in the slave states, showing it as a gang of violent men whose authority comes not from law but from terror.
ex_zh: The novel repeatedly shows posses as instruments of white solidarity across class lines: poor whites who would never socialize with planters eagerly join posse to hunt black bodies, revealing how racism unites the white population.

def_en: to search for and collect usable items from waste; to feed on carrion
def_zh: scavenge for food, scavenge scraps, scavenge through trash
quote_en: The refugees scavenged for food among the ruins of their former homes.
quote_zh: The slaves scavenged moss from the swamp trees and sold it in town, the meager coins their only currency in an economy that had already stolen everything else.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "scavenge" to describe the economic position of the enslaved: they are permitted to gather the leftovers of the plantation economy, and even those leftovers are subject to taxation or seizure.
ex_zh: The novel shows scavenging as both a literal survival activity and a metaphor for the way the enslaved must build lives from the discarded remnants of the master's world—a theme that extends to the underground railroad itself, which operates in the cracks and margins of the slave system.

def_en: a person who has escaped from captivity or is in hiding
def_zh: fugitive from justice, fugitive slave, fugitive hunter
quote_en: The police set up roadblocks to capture the fugitive who escaped from prison.
quote_zh: In Whitehead's novel, Cora becomes a fugitive on the underground railroad, hunted across state lines by the relentless slave catcher Ridgeway.
ex_en: The fugitive's journey transforms from a physical escape into a psychological reckoning with America's original sin, suggesting that freedom is not a destination but an endless flight from inherited trauma.
ex_zh: Whitehead's use of the word "fugitive" refuses the legalistic term "runaway slave," rehumanizing Cora by emphasizing her agency in flight rather than her status as stolen property.

def_en: a person's appearance or manner, especially as an indication of character
def_zh: dignified mien, somber mien, commanding mien
quote_en: The general had a commanding mien that inspired loyalty in his troops.
quote_zh: Ridgeway maintained a serene comportment at all times but generated a threatening atmosphere, his mien a thunderhead that seemed far away but then was suddenly overhead with loud violence.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "mien" to explore the performance of power: Ridgeway's calm exterior is not genuine serenity but a tool of intimidation, a mask that makes his violence more shocking.
ex_zh: The novel contrasts the mien of various authority figures (Ridgeway, Terrance, the night riders) to show that the slave system depends on a theatrical repertoire of faces—the stern master, the smiling patroller, the indifferent judge—each a role in a cruel play.

def_en: a person who supports or agrees with a particular cause or group
def_zh: rebel sympathizer, abolitionist sympathizer, secret sympathizer
quote_en: The government arrested anyone suspected of being a rebel sympathizer.
quote_zh: The underground railroad depended on white sympathizers like Fletcher, who risked lynching to harbor fugitives, yet even their bravery could not fully compensate for the terror that slavery had woven into every southern road and town.
ex_en: Whitehead complicates the figure of the white sympathizer by showing that even well-intentioned allies are limited by their own positionality—they can help individuals but cannot dismantle the system alone.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of Fletcher, Sam, and Martin shows that sympathizers are often motivated by a mix of genuine conviction and personal guilt, and their help, while essential, is never enough to make the fugitive truly safe.

def_en: to sail or travel all the way around something; to avoid
def_zh: circumnavigate the globe, circumnavigate an obstacle, circumnavigate a problem
quote_en: The explorer spent three years circumnavigating the globe.
quote_zh: On horses they circumnavigated the fields, comparing the progress of the harvest on the two halves of the Randall plantation, as if the enslaved workers below them were merely crops to be measured and weighed.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "circumnavigate" to describe the masters' relationship to the enslaved: they circle around them, inspect them, quantify them, but never see them as human.
ex_zh: The novel's structure, which takes Cora on a geographical circumnavigation of the South (Georgia, South Carolina, North Carolina, Tennessee, Indiana), suggests that escaping the plantation requires not just movement but a complete reorientation of how one sees the land and its people.

def_en: a person who is guided and supported by an older or more experienced person
def_zh: mentor and protégé, young protégé, musical protégé
quote_en: The famous sculptor took the young artist as his protégé, teaching her everything he knew.
quote_zh: Terrance Randall had no protégés, only property he broke and discarded; his father's old allies avoided him, for his cruelty had no purpose beyond its own satisfaction.
ex_en: Whitehead shows that the master-protégé relationship is impossible under slavery because genuine mentorship requires recognizing the humanity of the other, which the master cannot do.
ex_zh: The novel contrasts the false protégé relationship (Terrance and his favorites) with the genuine one (Royal guiding Cora), suggesting that freedom is learned not from masters but from fellow fugitives.

def_en: a secret meeting, especially between lovers
def_zh: clandestine assignation, assignation with a lover, assignation spot
quote_en: The novel begins with a clandestine assignation in a rain-soaked garden.
quote_zh: The rotting schoolhouse on the Randall plantation was used for assignations and all those different lessons—places where slaves snatched moments of intimacy before the overseer's whistle called them back to the rows.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "assignation" to capture the furtive, stolen quality of slave intimacy, where even private meetings are subject to interruption and punishment.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of assignations reveals that the plantation cannot fully control the bodies of the enslaved, even as it claims total ownership; the schoolhouse assignation is a small but significant site of resistance.

def_en: respected and impressive; majestic
def_zh: august body, august presence, august occasion
quote_en: The august assembly of world leaders gathered to sign the treaty.
quote_zh: A band of august souls from Atlanta and Savannah arrived in a carriage to witness Big Anthony's torture, savoring turtle soup and mutton while a man was whipped for their entertainment.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "august" ironically to describe the white elites whose dignity is built on the suffering of the enslaved—their august status is a mask for barbarism.
ex_zh: The novel forces readers to see that the august institutions of the Old South—the legislature, the judiciary, the church—were all complicit in the torture of human beings, and their augustness is a lie maintained by violence.

def_en: a person who supported the end of slavery
def_zh: radical abolitionist, abolitionist movement, abolitionist literature
quote_en: Frederick Douglass was the most famous abolitionist of his era.
quote_zh: Fletcher had never been active in abolitionist circles up north, but observing the monstrous system firsthand in Georgia gave him thoughts he did not recognize—thoughts that could get him run out of town or worse.
ex_en: Whitehead presents abolitionists as a mixed group: some are genuine heroes, others are motivated by guilt or self-interest, but all are essential to the underground railroad.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that true abolitionism requires not just intellectual conviction but direct action—Fletcher, Sam, and Martin all risk their lives because they have seen slavery firsthand, and that seeing compels them to act.

def_en: truthfulness; accuracy
def_zh: question the veracity, veracity of the claim, veracity of testimony
quote_en: The journalist questioned the veracity of the government's official statement.
quote_zh: Caesar had met earnest white men like Fletcher before, who believed what came out of their mouths; the veracity of their words was another matter, but at least they believed them.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "veracity" to explore the unreliability of testimony in a slave society, where even well-meaning whites may be blind to their own complicity.
ex_zh: The novel's narrative voice, which shifts between omniscient third-person and limited perspectives, constantly questions the veracity of any single account, suggesting that the truth of slavery is too vast for any one storyteller to capture.

def_en: to go or travel along a winding course
def_zh: wend one's way, wend through the crowd, wend homeward
quote_en: The pilgrims wended their way through the narrow streets toward the cathedral.
quote_zh: The three fugitives wended west through the black water of the swamp, tromping through peat and silt, following Caesar's memory of islands of sure ground that might lead them north.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wend" to evoke the slow, uncertain, meandering quality of escape—the underground railroad is not a straight line but a winding path through dangerous territory.
ex_zh: The novel's structure wends through different states and narrative modes, refusing a simple linear progression, just as Cora's escape wends through swamps, tunnels, and false promises before reaching any kind of resolution.

def_en: the object of a hunt; a large animal being hunted
def_zh: pursue one's quarry, elusive quarry, hunter and quarry
quote_en: The hounds pursued their quarry through the dense underbrush.
quote_zh: The hog hunters had laid bait on the trail, waiting for their quarry to turn nocturnal in the hot weather; instead of wild pigs, they found three runaways—a different sort of beast but more remunerative.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "quarry" to blur the line between human and animal, showing how the slave system classifies fugitives as game to be hunted.
ex_zh: The novel's many hunting scenes (the patrollers, the posses, Ridgeway) force readers to see the fugitive as quarry, but also to recognize that the hunters are the true beasts, their humanity forfeited by their cruelty.

def_en: financially rewarding; profitable
def_zh: remunerative employment, remunerative venture, highly remunerative
quote_en: She left her teaching position for a more remunerative career in finance.
quote_zh: The hog hunters were disappointed to find runaways instead of pigs, but capturing fugitives proved even more remunerative than selling wild game to the local markets.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "remunerative" to expose the economic logic that drives slave catching: human beings are a source of profit, their capture a business transaction.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that the entire slave system is remunerative for some, but that profit is built on the commodification of human life—a calculation that deforms everyone involved, from the masters to the patrollers to the gawkers at the auction.

def_en: something that is imagined or invented
def_zh: figment of the imagination, mere figment, figment and fantasy
quote_en: The monster turned out to be a figment of the child's imagination.
quote_zh: Time was a figment in the chaos of the escape; Cora could not tell how long she had been fighting, only that Caesar was calling her name and pulling her roughly forward into the darkness.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "figment" to suggest that the normal measurements of reality (time, space, identity) become unreliable in the fugitive's experience, dissolving into subjective perception.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of memory and trauma shows that the past is not a figment but a living presence—Ajarry's experience lives on in Cora, and the figments of freedom that slaves imagine are often more real than the brutal facts of their bondage.

def_en: to align or position something relative to a direction
def_zh: orient oneself, orient toward, orient around
quote_en: She needed a few days to orient herself in the new city.
quote_zh: Caesar oriented himself with the constellations, using the stars to guide the runaways through the unfamiliar forest after they had lost their provisions and lost Lovey to the night.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "orient" to emphasize the importance of knowing one's position—the fugitive must constantly reorient, as the landmarks of the plantation (the big house, the quarters) are replaced by the stars, the swamp, the tunnel.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that freedom requires a complete reorientation of one's moral compass: the values of the plantation (obedience, submission, silence) must be abandoned for the fugitive's values (resistance, flight, solidarity).

def_en: to roam in search of plunder or goods to steal
def_zh: marauding band, maraud through, maraud and pillage
quote_en: Barbarian tribes marauded across the countryside, burning villages in their path.
quote_zh: Patrol bands marauded through the slave villages and ransacked the homes of freemen, stealing and committing assaults, using the search for two escaped slaves as cover for every cruelty they had ever wished to inflict.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "maraud" to describe state-sanctioned violence, showing that the line between lawful authority and criminal gang is thin in a slave society.
ex_zh: The novel's marauding posses and patrollers reveal that the slave system cannot maintain order through law alone; it requires periodic outbursts of uncontrolled violence that terrorize even compliant slaves.

def_en: the protective care of God or nature; timely preparation
def_zh: divine providence, act of providence, trust in providence
quote_en: By providence, the ship arrived just before the hurricane struck.
quote_zh: Providence smiled on the fugitives when the hunters believed they hid in the swamp, allowing them to get farther northeast than any runaways from the Randall plantation had ever traveled.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "providence" ironically: the fugitives succeed not because of divine intervention but because of human error and their own ingenuity, yet they interpret their luck as providence.
ex_zh: The novel questions the role of providence in the slave's escape: if God is just, why does he not help all fugitives? The answer seems to be that the underground railroad is a human, not divine, creation, and its successes are owed to human courage.

def_en: a metal band for restraining the hands; to confine
def_zh: manacle and chain, manacle the wrists, manacle of convention
quote_en: The guards manacled the prisoner's wrists behind his back.
quote_zh: Thousands of manacles dangled off the wall in Lumbly's barn—shackles for ankles and wrists and necks in all varieties, including children's chains with tiny manacles that testified to an industry of human cruelty.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "manacle" as a synecdoche for the entire slave system: the small metal device represents the massive machinery of control that binds human beings.
ex_zh: The novel's description of the manacle collection in Lumbly's barn is a museum of suffering, each set of irons a story of capture and confinement, and the children's manacles are the most devastating exhibit, showing that the system enslaves even the smallest bodies.

def_en: to leave hurriedly and secretly, typically to escape from custody
def_zh: abscond with funds, abscond from justice, abscond into the night
quote_en: The accountant absconded with millions of dollars from the company.
quote_zh: Jasper absconded from the Florida cane fields and made it to Tennessee before a tinker caught him stealing food from his pantry, his brief taste of freedom ending not in Canada but in chains beside Cora in Ridgeway's wagon.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "abscond" to emphasize the secrecy and speed required for escape, but also the desperation: most absconding slaves are caught, and their punishment is more severe than if they had never tried.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of absconding shows that the decision to run is not a calculation of success but a refusal to continue accepting the unacceptable—even a failed abscond is an assertion of humanity.

def_en: a characteristic rising and falling rhythm; to speak in a cheerful or musical manner
def_zh: Irish lilt, voice lilt, melodic lilt
quote_en: Her voice had a gentle Irish lilt that made everyone feel at ease.
quote_zh: The station agent Lumbly had an odd manner of speech, a queer lilt that reminded Cora of the way those on the plantation sounded after they lost their wits—as if madness and sanity were merely different dialects of the same language.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lilt" to suggest that the underground railroad attracts eccentric, marginal figures whose strangeness is a form of resistance to the norms of a slave society.
ex_zh: The novel's cast of characters (Lumbly, Sam, Homer, Royal) all have a certain lilt—a way of speaking or being that refuses to fit into the categories of master or slave, suggesting that freedom requires a kind of joyful madness.

def_en: a benefit or privilege received in addition to regular payment
def_zh: executive perquisites, perquisite of office, perquisite and privilege
quote_en: The company car was a perquisite of the executive position.
quote_zh: The patrollers enjoyed certain perquisites apart from the thrill of shaming a man in front of his family—the Mutter farm had the comeliest colored wenches, and a roust allowed them to replenish their whiskey jars.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "perquisite" to expose the corruption of the slave-catching system: the patrollers are not disinterested enforcers of law but predators who use their authority for personal gain.
ex_zh: The novel's perquisites—whiskey, women, violence—reveal that the slave system rewards the worst instincts of the poor white population, turning them into willing tools of the planter class.

def_en: outstandingly bad; shocking
def_zh: egregious error, egregious violation, egregious behavior
quote_en: The referee made an egregious error that cost the team the championship.
quote_zh: The other patrollers were boys and men of bad character, but Ridgeway commanded his appetites in those days, withdrawing before his confederates' more egregious displays of violence and lust.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "egregious" to describe acts so terrible that they stand out even in a system built on routine cruelty—Ridgeway's relative restraint is not virtue but calculation.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that the most egregious acts of violence are not the exceptions but the logical conclusions of a system that treats human beings as property; the only surprise is that more masters are not as egregious as Terrance Randall.

def_en: a miserly person who avoids spending money
def_zh: penny-pinching skinflint, old skinflint, skinflint and miser
quote_en: The old skinflint refused to donate even a single dollar to the charity.
quote_zh: An absconded slave might fetch as little as two dollars if the owner was a skinflint, or as much as a hundred dollars if the master was generous and the capture required crossing state lines.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "skinflint" to show that even the miserliness of masters affects the lives of the enslaved—a stingy master pays lower rewards, making fugitives less valuable and more likely to be killed.
ex_zh: The novel's economic language (skinflint, liquidate, remunerative) reveals that the slave system is a market like any other, with supply and demand determining the value of human life—a calculus that deforms everyone it touches.

def_en: obtrusively bright and showy; lurid
def_zh: garish colors, garish display, garish and gaudy
quote_en: The casino's garish neon lights flashed through the night.
quote_zh: Even the shortest ferry ride delivered Ridgeway to a new island nation, garish and imposing, each town more lunatic and complicated than the last as he traveled north in pursuit of fugitives.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "garish" to describe the North as seen through Ridgeway's hostile eyes—its colors are too bright, its freedoms too loud, its complexity an affront to his orderly vision of racial hierarchy.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of the North as garish suggests that freedom can be disorienting, even ugly, and that the line between the free states and the slave states is not as clear as the fugitives hope—the North has its own garish horrors.

def_en: a rich fabric woven with raised patterns, often metallic thread
def_zh: gold brocade, brocade fabric, brocade gown
quote_en: The queen's gown was made of gold brocade and trimmed with ermine.
quote_zh: The twenty dollars from his first capture went to new boots and a brocade coat like he'd seen some worthies wear in Washington, D.C.—a coat his belly would outgrow before many years had passed.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "brocade" to symbolize the aspirational costume of the slave catcher, who dresses like a gentleman but remains a killer—the fine fabric cannot掩盖 his brutality.
ex_zh: The novel's attention to clothing (brocade coats, silk tobacco pouches, negro cloth) shows how material culture reflects and reinforces social hierarchies, and how the enslaved use clothing to disguise themselves and escape.

def_en: enormous; of immense size
def_zh: gargantuan task, gargantuan appetite, gargantuan scale
quote_en: The project required a gargantuan effort from the entire team.
quote_zh: New York City was a gargantuan metropolis where the fugitive trade, the liberty movement, and the ingenuity of the colored community all converged to portray the true scale of the hunt.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gargantuan" to convey the overwhelming size and complexity of the North, which is both a refuge and a labyrinth where fugitives can get lost.
ex_zh: The novel's gargantuan cities (New York, Philadelphia, Boston) are presented as alternatives to the plantation, but they are also sites of capture, as Ridgeway's success there shows—size does not guarantee safety.

def_en: a person employed to load and unload ships
def_zh: dock stevedore, stevedore labor, stevedore union
quote_en: The stevedores worked through the night to unload the cargo from Africa.
quote_zh: Sympathizers and mercenary captains smuggled fugitives into the city ports, while stevedores and dockhands and clerks furnished Ridgeway with information, and he scooped up the rascals on the threshold of deliverance.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "stevedore" to show that the underground railroad depends on ordinary working people, whose daily labor makes escape possible—but also that some of those workers betray fugitives for profit.
ex_zh: The novel's stevedores are like the patrollers: they occupy a liminal space between the enslaved and the free, and their choices determine whether a fugitive reaches freedom or is dragged back to the plantation.

def_en: the act of being rescued or set free
def_zh: prayer for deliverance, deliverance from evil, day of deliverance
quote_en: The hostages prayed for deliverance from their captors.
quote_zh: On the threshold of deliverance, with freedom just yards away, many fugitives were captured—their dreams of Canada shattered by a paid informant or a patroller who happened to glance the wrong way.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "deliverance" to name the fugitive's goal, but also to emphasize its fragility: deliverance is never guaranteed, and the moment of rescue is also the moment of greatest danger.
ex_zh: The novel's many near-deliverances (Cora in South Carolina, Cora in the attic, Cora on the train) create a pattern of hope and disappointment that mirrors the historical experience of the underground railroad, where deliverance was always provisional.

def_en: an opening strategy designed to gain an advantage
def_zh: opening gambit, chess gambit, strategic gambit
quote_en: Her sudden question was a gambit to catch him off guard.
quote_zh: High-minded idiocy pitted against the power of coin: abolitionist lawyers erected barricades of paperwork, while Ridgeway's gambit was simply to bribe the city recorder to tip him to freshly jailed fugitives.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gambit" to describe the tactical moves in the cat-and-mouse game between abolitionists and slave catchers—each side has its gambits, and the fugitives are the pieces being moved.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that the underground railroad is a kind of chess game, with gambits and counter-gambits, but the stakes are human lives, and the players are not evenly matched—the slave catchers have the law and the money on their side.

def_en: the entrails and internal organs of an animal used as food; refuse
def_zh: offal and refuse, meat offal, offal disposal
quote_en: The butcher sold the offal to specialty markets at a low price.
quote_zh: The gutters of New York City overflowed with offal and refuse, but the mess found its place in time—just as the dirty white flood of European immigrants had nowhere to go but out, south, west, following the same laws that governed garbage and people.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "offal" to draw a parallel between the treatment of garbage and the treatment of people: both are swept out of sight, both are considered waste, and both are subject to the same economic laws.
ex_zh: The novel's offal imagery (the gutters, the swamps, the burning bodies) suggests that America's treatment of its unwanted populations—whether slaves, Indians, or poor immigrants—follows the same logic: they are offal to be disposed of, not people to be valued.

def_en: the undermining of authority or established systems
def_zh: subversion of authority, subversion of order, subversion and sedition
quote_en: The government accused the newspaper of publishing materials that encouraged subversion.
quote_zh: The underground railroad maintained no lines to speak of, yet they openly bragged of their subversion, hustling a slave out the back door as the slave catchers broke down the front—a criminal conspiracy devoted to theft of property.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "subversion" to name the underground railroad's project: not just helping individuals escape but undermining the entire system of slavery by proving it can be defeated.
ex_zh: The novel's subversive structure—turning the railroad, a symbol of American progress, into a secret network of escape—suggests that true subversion comes from reimagining the nation's founding myths and technologies for liberatory purposes.

def_en: a refutation or contradiction; a counterargument
def_zh: strong rebuttal, legal rebuttal, rebuttal evidence
quote_en: The scientist published a detailed rebuttal to the study's flawed methodology.
quote_zh: The abolitionist wing of the Methodists circulated Carter's pamphlets on Sunday morning, and London periodicals published his arguments without rebuttal—a fact that galled Ridgeway every time he thought of it.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "rebuttal" to describe the rhetorical battle over slavery: the abolitionists offer moral rebuttals, the slaveholders offer violence, and the two never truly meet.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that no rebuttal, however eloquent, can defeat the slave system because the system is not based on argument but on force; the only effective rebuttal is escape.

def_en: a decorative molding at the top of a building or wall
def_zh: white cornice, cornice of a building, carved cornice
quote_en: The ancient temple's marble cornice had been worn smooth by centuries of rain.
quote_zh: From the window seat in the Andersons' house, Bessie could just make out the top two floors of the Griffin Building, with its white cornices that blazed in the sunlight like a promise of everything the South Carolina experiment claimed to offer.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "cornice" as a symbol of the false promise of South Carolina's colored uplift program: the beautiful building hides the sterilization experiments inside.
ex_zh: The novel's architectural details (cornices, dormitories, the Griffin Building) show how the built environment reflects and reinforces racial hierarchies—beautiful cornices can adorn buildings where black bodies are being violated, just as fine clothes can conceal brutality.

def_en: decorated with ruffles or fringes; overly ornate
def_zh: frilly dress, frilly decoration, frilly and feminine
quote_en: The little girl wore a frilly pink dress to the wedding.
quote_zh: The dressmaker displayed her frilly, colorful creations draped on hooped wire, and Bessie never failed to linger by the big glass windows, marveling at goods she could not afford and colors she had never imagined.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "frilly" to represent the consumer goods that define freedom for Bessie in South Carolina—pretty things that distract her from the fact that her body is still controlled by the state.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of frilly dresses and other luxury items suggests that consumer culture can be a form of captivity, luring the newly free into debt and dependency while offering the illusion of autonomy.

def_en: a large retail store selling a wide variety of goods
def_zh: department emporium, emporium of goods, shopping emporium
quote_en: The old emporium on Main Street sold everything from farm tools to candy.
quote_zh: The overstuffed emporiums of South Carolina displayed a wonderland of goods, their shelves abundant with items that Cora had never seen on the plantation—each one a small testament to a world beyond the cotton rows.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "emporium" to represent the seductive abundance of consumer capitalism, which promises freedom through purchase but actually creates new forms of dependence.
ex_zh: The novel's emporiums are the commercial counterpart to the museum: both display goods and people, both arrange the world for white consumption, and both teach the colored population their place in the hierarchy.

def_en: a densely populated or maze-like area
def_zh: rabbit warren, warren of alleys, warren of offices
quote_en: The old city was a warren of narrow alleys and hidden courtyards.
quote_zh: Mr. Anderson's floor in the Griffin Building was a warren of lawyers' offices, with rich carpets, walls of dark brown wood, and doors inlaid with frosted glass—a labyrinth of commerce where men in suits decided the fates of people they would never meet.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "warren" to describe the spaces where power is exercised: hidden, maze-like, inaccessible to the people whose lives are decided there.
ex_zh: The novel's warrens (the Griffin Building, the underground tunnels, the plantation quarters) are all spaces where the enslaved or the poor are excluded, and where decisions are made that affect them without their knowledge or consent.

def_en: the customers or patrons of a business
def_zh: loyal clientele, upscale clientele, clientele base
quote_en: The exclusive restaurant attracted a wealthy clientele from across the city.
quote_zh: Bessie crossed the street to avoid the jumble of saloons and their disreputable clientele, men who might recognize her as a runaway or simply decide that a colored woman walking alone was fair game for harassment.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "clientele" to show how public spaces are segregated by class and race, and how the clientele of a saloon can be a threat to any black woman who passes by.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of clientele suggests that freedom of movement is not guaranteed by law but by the willingness of the white clientele to tolerate your presence—and that tolerance can vanish at any moment.

def_en: wild, untamed, or having returned to a wild state
def_zh: feral child, feral animal, feral gaze
quote_en: The abandoned cat had become feral, hissing at anyone who approached.
quote_zh: The boy's shining eyes, set in his round pudgy face, were at once feral and serene—a kindred spirit, Ridgeway thought, worth five dollars and the inconvenience of drawing up emancipation papers.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "feral" to describe Homer, the strange black boy who serves Ridgeway—he is wild, untamed, but also eerily calm, as if he has transcended the normal categories of human emotion.
ex_zh: The novel's feral characters (Homer, the Hob women, the maroons) exist outside the master-slave binary, inhabiting a liminal space that is both dangerous and free—they are neither properly enslaved nor properly free, but something else entirely.

def_en: not spoiled or stained; pure
def_zh: unsullied reputation, unsullied virtue, unsullied white
quote_en: The mountain snow remained unsullied by the city's pollution.
quote_zh: The brick of the dormitories was a pure, unsullied color, without so much as a dot of mud splashed from the rain—a perfection that seemed impossible on a plantation, where everything was stained by the labor of enslaved hands.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "unsullied" to describe the South Carolina dormitories' appearance, which is a lie—the unsullied brick hides the unspeakable experiments conducted inside.
ex_zh: The novel's attention to surfaces that appear unsullied (whitewashed cabins, clean dormitories, neat towns) reveals that the slave system invests enormous energy in maintaining the appearance of order and purity, but beneath that surface is the stain of violence and exploitation.

def_en: to slow down or reduce speed
def_zh: decelerate quickly, decelerate to a stop, decelerate the vehicle
quote_en: The train began to decelerate as it approached the station.
quote_zh: The locomotive decelerated and Caesar jumped up, unable to believe that they had completed another leg of their journey—each deceleration a small death, each acceleration a rebirth into a new state they could not yet imagine.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "decelerate" to describe the rhythm of the underground railroad, where moments of rest are always followed by renewed flight—the fugitive can never stop decelerating, never settle.
ex_zh: The novel's structure of acceleration and deceleration mirrors the fugitive's psychological state: the terror of the chase, the brief respite of a station, the dread of the next departure—all are part of a rhythm that has no final cadence.

def_en: destruction or ruin; to cause suffering
def_zh: wrack and ruin, wracked with pain, wracked by guilt
quote_en: The coastal town was left in wrack after the hurricane passed.
quote_zh: The engineer mopped sweat and soot from his face and was about to speak when a ferocious coughing wracked his person, the price of spending his life underground, ferrying fugitives through darkness toward a light he might never see.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wrack" to describe the physical toll of the underground railroad on its workers—their bodies are wracked by disease, exhaustion, and the constant terror of discovery.
ex_zh: The novel's engineers, station agents, and conductors are all wracked by the work they do, but they continue because the alternative—allowing the slave system to continue unchallenged—is worse than any physical suffering.

def_en: food or provisions
def_zh: victuals and supplies, cook victuals, store victuals
quote_en: The travelers packed enough victuals to last the entire journey.
quote_zh: The basket contained victuals—bread, half a chicken, water, and a bottle of beer—and the runaways were so hungry they shook out the crumbs from the basket to divvy, each crumb a small victory over the starvation that had followed them from Georgia.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "victuals" to emphasize the material conditions of escape: fugitives are always hungry, always rationing, always at the edge of starvation—food is as precious as freedom.
ex_zh: The novel's attention to victuals (the yams Cora digs up, the bread in the station baskets, the meager rations on the train) shows that the underground railroad is not just a network of tunnels but a logistical operation that must feed its passengers, and that the simplest victuals can mean the difference between life and death.

def_en: to divide or share something
def_zh: divvy up, divvy the spoils, divvy among
quote_en: The thieves agreed to divvy the loot equally among themselves.
quote_zh: They were so hungry they shook out the crumbs from the basket to divvy, each morsel measured with the precision of a plantation overseer weighing cotton at the end of a long day.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "divvy" to show how fugitives must share scarce resources, creating a miniature communist economy based on mutual aid and survival.
ex_zh: The novel's divvying scenes contrast with the plantation's distribution of rations, where the master decides who gets what—the fugitives' divvying is an act of self-determination and solidarity.

def_en: a small detail or point of law
def_zh: legal technicality, technicality of the law, on a technicality
quote_en: The case was dismissed on a technicality, not on its merits.
quote_zh: "It says here we're the property of the United States Government," Caesar pointed out, but Sam explained that it was a technicality—white families flocked to South Carolina for opportunities, and the colored were bought up by the government in a migration the country had never witnessed before.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "technicality" to expose the legal fictions that underpin the slave system—the claim that the government owns the colored population is a technicality that can be ignored when convenient and enforced when useful.
ex_zh: The novel's many legal technicalities (the Fugitive Slave Law, the bills of sale, the emancipation papers) show that the law is not a neutral arbiter of justice but a tool of oppression that can be manipulated to serve the powerful.

def_en: a woman with whom secrets are shared
def_zh: trusted confidante, close confidante, confidante and advisor
quote_en: The queen's only confidante was her younger sister.
quote_zh: A confidante in the Griffin Building had forged their papers, but Cora knew that a confidante was also a liability—one whispered word to the wrong ear, and the underground railroad would collapse like a tunnel hit by a cave-in.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "confidante" to highlight the importance of trust in the underground railroad: the entire network depends on confidantes who keep secrets, but every confidante is also a potential informant.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of confidantes (Miss Lucy, the Anderson's maid, Fiona) shows that intimacy and betrayal are two sides of the same coin in a slave society, where even the most trusted confidante can turn you in for a reward.

def_en: an elementary textbook for teaching children to read
def_zh: reading primer, primer for beginners, primer lesson
quote_en: The child struggled through the first pages of her primer, sounding out each letter.
quote_zh: Cora removed her primer from her trunk and, while the other girls gossiped, practiced her letters—the next time she signed for the Andersons' groceries, she would write Bessie in careful print, each letter a declaration that she was no longer the illiterate girl who had fled Georgia in chains.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "primer" to symbolize the education that the slave system forbids—learning to read is an act of rebellion, and the primer is a weapon in the struggle for freedom.
ex_zh: The novel's primers (Cora's, the children's at the Valentine farm, the library books) represent the promise of self-improvement and liberation through literacy, but also the danger that reading will give the enslaved ideas above their station.

def_en: patient self-control; tolerance
def_zh: forbearance and patience, exercise forbearance, judicial forbearance
quote_en: The teacher showed great forbearance with her most difficult students.
quote_zh: The families were told to exercise forbearance with inexperienced help, for many of the colored women had never worked in a white household before and had to learn not only the tasks but the posture of deference that survival required.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "forbearance" to describe the attitude white employers are asked to adopt toward their colored servants—forbearance is a form of paternalism that still assumes the superiority of the white person.
ex_zh: The novel suggests that forbearance is not kindness but a strategy of control: the master's forbearance can be withdrawn at any moment, and the servant who relies on it is never safe.

def_en: to groom oneself with elaborate care
def_zh: primp before a mirror, primp and preen, primp for a date
quote_en: She spent an hour primping before the party, checking her hair from every angle.
quote_zh: The girls in the bunk room were in a state of great excitement over the evening's gathering, primping before the small mirror, each one hoping that tonight might be the night a young man looked at her and saw not a slave but a woman.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "primp" to show how the colored women of South Carolina try to assert their humanity through appearance, even as their bodies are subject to state control and medical experimentation.
ex_zh: The novel's primping scenes are poignant because the readers know that the women's efforts to look beautiful will not protect them from the doctors' scalpels or the slave catchers' chains.

def_en: an uncouth or aggressive man
def_zh: drunken lout, lout and boor, loutish behavior
quote_en: The drunken lout was asked to leave the tavern after shouting at the bartender.
quote_zh: One of the louts at the tavern gave Sam a black eye, and he was proud of it, saying he'd always wanted one—as if every bruise and scar he accumulated in the service of the underground railroad was a medal rather than a wound.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lout" to describe the kind of men who become patrollers and slave catchers—not the planters, but the poor whites whose brutality is their only form of power.
ex_zh: The novel's louts (the hog hunters, the night riders, the posse members) are the foot soldiers of the slave system, men whose violence is rewarded and whose loutishness is normalized by a society that needs them to terrorize the enslaved.

def_en: having developed certain abilities at an earlier age than usual
def_zh: precocious child, precocious talent, precocious intelligence
quote_en: The precocious child was reading novels by the age of seven.
quote_zh: Lander demonstrated his brilliance from an early age, a precocious child who made the family library his playground, poring over volumes he struggled to lift from the shelves—a mind forged in privilege that he would dedicate to the liberation of those who had no such advantages.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "precocious" to describe Lander, whose early genius is both a gift and a burden—he sees the world more clearly than others, but that clarity cannot save him from the bullet that kills him.
ex_zh: The novel's precocious characters (Lander, Homer, Chester) are all marked by their intelligence, but their precocity does not protect them from the violence of the slave system—it may even make them more vulnerable, as the powerful fear the smart slave.

def_en: required by law or rules; compulsory
def_zh: mandatory attendance, mandatory requirement, mandatory minimum
quote_en: Wearing a seatbelt is mandatory in most countries.
quote_zh: Dr. Stevens explained that the sterilization surgery was mandatory for some in the state—colored women with more than two children, imbeciles, habitual criminals—as if the bodies of the unfit were public property, to be altered for the convenience of the state.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "mandatory" to expose the eugenic logic of South Carolina's colored uplift program: the state decides which bodies are fit to reproduce, and mandatory sterilization is the tool of that decision.
ex_zh: The novel's mandatory procedures (the blood tests, the examinations, the surgeries) show that the line between medicine and torture, between care and control, is thin indeed when the patients are black and the doctors are white.

def_en: stubbornly disobedient; resistant to authority
def_zh: recalcitrant patient, recalcitrant student, recalcitrant employee
quote_en: The recalcitrant student refused to follow any classroom rules.
quote_zh: She wasn't his first recalcitrant patient, Dr. Stevens said, putting aside the matter of sterilization without losing his warm demeanor—as if Cora's resistance to having her future cut out of her was merely a medical inconvenience to be managed with patience.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "recalcitrant" to describe the attitude of those who refuse to submit to the state's control over their bodies—recalcitrance is a form of resistance, and the state treats it as a pathology to be cured.
ex_zh: The novel's recalcitrant characters (Cora, Caesar, the woman who screams about her babies) are all punished for their refusal to comply, but their recalcitrance is also what makes them fugitives—the compliant slave never runs.

def_en: without suffering any injury or damage
def_zh: escape unscathed, emerge unscathed, survive unscathed
quote_en: The driver walked away from the car accident completely unscathed.
quote_zh: Cora had become too accustomed to escaping unscathed from encounters with white authority, but Dr. Stevens's questions and the talk of mandatory sterilization reminded her that no encounter with the white man's power was ever truly without cost.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "unscathed" to question the possibility of escaping the slave system without damage—even if the body is unscathed, the psyche bears scars that never fully heal.
ex_zh: The novel's survivors (Cora, the Valentine farm refugees, the Hob women) are all marked by their experiences, and the idea of emerging unscathed is a fantasy—the best one can hope for is to survive with enough of oneself intact to keep running.

def_en: accorded a great deal of respect, especially because of age or wisdom
def_zh: venerable elder, venerable institution, venerable tradition
quote_en: The venerable professor had taught at the university for fifty years.
quote_zh: Jockey was the oldest colored man anyone on the Randall plantations had ever met, a venerable survivor who had outlived every torment big and small white men had concocted and implemented—his very existence a form of rebellion.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "venerable" to describe Jockey, whose age commands respect even from masters who would otherwise see him as worthless—his longevity is a testament to his resilience and a rebuke to the system that tried to destroy him.
ex_zh: The novel's venerable figures (Jockey, Old Abraham, the Hob women) are revered not for their wisdom alone but for having survived—in a system designed to kill you, simply living to old age is an act of heroic resistance.

def_en: a state of disorder or confusion
def_zh: in disarray, thrown into disarray, complete disarray
quote_en: The office was in complete disarray after the late-night party.
quote_zh: She had been too distracted to notice the house's disarray on her previous visit, the grimed plates, sawdust, and piles of clothes—the detritus of a man who had sacrificed his domestic peace for the dangerous work of the underground railroad.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "disarray" to describe the personal lives of those who work for the underground railroad—their commitment to the cause leaves no room for order or comfort, and their homes reflect the chaos of their vocation.
ex_zh: The novel's disarray (Sam's messy cottage, Martin's cluttered attic, the abandoned stations) shows that the fight against slavery is not clean or orderly—it is messy, dangerous, and all-consuming.

def_en: a strong desire or longing
def_zh: hankering for adventure, hankering after fame, hankering for the past
quote_en: Despite his success, he still had a hankering for the simpler life of his childhood.
quote_zh: Sam's regular was a doctor by the name of Bertram who had a hankering for whiskey, drinking to drown out his sins—the sins of performing experiments on colored patients who believed they were being helped, their bodies reduced to data in a study they would never understand.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "hankering" to describe the doctor's alcoholism, which is a response to the guilt of his medical atrocities—his hankering for whiskey is a symptom of a diseased conscience.
ex_zh: The novel's hankerings (for whiskey, for freedom, for revenge) are all expressions of lack, and the way characters satisfy or suppress their hankerings reveals their moral character.

def_en: gloomy, morose, or sullen in temperament
def_zh: saturnine disposition, saturnine expression, saturnine mood
quote_en: The old man had a saturnine disposition that discouraged conversation.
quote_zh: The doctor preferred Miss Trumball's establishment to the Lanchester House, whose girls had a saturnine disposition in his opinion, as if imported from Maine or other gloom-loving provinces—as if women's bodies existed only for his convenience and pleasure.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "saturnine" to describe the prostitutes whom the doctor rejects, revealing his misogyny and his expectation that women should be cheerful and available for his use.
ex_zh: The novel's saturnine characters (the doctor, Ridgeway, Terrance) are all men who project their own gloom onto others, blaming their victims for the darkness in their own souls.

def_en: inclined or susceptible to something beforehand
def_zh: genetically predisposed, predisposed to illness, predisposed to violence
quote_en: Some people are genetically predisposed to certain diseases.
quote_zh: Did Sam know that the Igbo tribe of the African continent is predisposed to nervous disorders, suicide and black moods? The doctor's question revealed how easily science could be twisted to justify the destruction of those deemed "unfit."
ex_en: Whitehead uses "predisposed" to expose the pseudo-scientific racism that underlies eugenics—the claim that certain races are predisposed to certain traits is used to justify their elimination.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of predisposition (the doctor's theories, the sterilization program, the medical experiments) shows how science can be corrupted to serve the interests of power, turning human beings into data points and predispositions into death sentences.

def_en: feeling or expressing pensive sadness
def_zh: melancholic mood, melancholic temperament, melancholic music
quote_en: The melancholic painting depicted a lone figure gazing at a distant sea.
quote_zh: What if we performed adjustments to the niggers' breeding patterns and removed those of melancholic tendency? The doctor's casual suggestion of eugenic engineering revealed the cold logic that underpinned South Carolina's "benevolent" system.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "melancholic" to show how the state pathologizes normal human emotions in the colored population—sadness is not a response to oppression but a trait to be bred out.
ex_zh: The novel's melancholic characters (the Hob women, the woman who screams about her babies, the victims of sterilization) are not suffering from a genetic predisposition but from the very real trauma of being treated as subhuman.

def_en: capable of being transmitted from one person to another
def_zh: communicable disease, communicable illness, communicable infection
quote_en: Measles is a highly communicable disease that spreads through respiratory droplets.
quote_zh: Controlled sterilization, research into communicable diseases, the perfection of new surgical techniques on the socially unfit—was it any wonder the best medical talents in the country were flocking to South Carolina?
ex_en: Whitehead uses "communicable" to describe the diseases that the state uses as justification for medical experimentation on the colored population—the research is framed as public health, but it is really eugenics.
ex_zh: The novel's communicable diseases (syphilis, yellow fever, the plague) are all used as excuses to violate the bodies of the enslaved and the poor, revealing that public health can be a mask for state violence.

def_en: not able to be corrected or reformed
def_zh: incorrigible criminal, incorrigible behavior, incorrigible optimist
quote_en: The judge sentenced the incorrigible youth to a reformatory for repeat offenders.
quote_zh: The worst sort of scoundrels took up the chase for the escaped slaves—drunkards, incorrigibles, poor whites who didn't even own shoes, delighted in this opportunity to scourge the colored population.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "incorrigible" to describe the poor whites who become patrollers—they are considered incorrigible by respectable society, but their brutality is useful to the planters.
ex_zh: The novel's incorrigibles (the hog hunters, the night riders, the louts) are the disposable enforcers of the slave system, men whose incorrigibility is tolerated because it serves the interests of power.

def_en: strong hostility or ill feeling
def_zh: personal animus, racial animus, animus toward
quote_en: The debate was conducted without personal animus, despite the strong disagreements.
quote_zh: Cora didn't know the source of the red-haired woman's animus as she scowled at the slave display, but she didn't care—she stared into the woman's eyes until she broke, a small victory in a war that would never end.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "animus" to describe the irrational hatred that white people direct at the colored population—an animus that is not based on anything the individual has done but on the color of their skin.
ex_zh: The novel's animus (the red-haired woman, the night riders, the mob in North Carolina) is not personal but systemic—it is the hatred that the slave system breeds in those who benefit from it, a hatred that cannot be reasoned away.

def_en: feeling strained and harassed; troubled
def_zh: harried mother, harried clerk, harried expression
quote_en: The harried mother tried to calm her three crying children while answering the phone.
quote_zh: Cora selected one patron per hour to evil-eye—a young clerk, a harried matron, a sour youth who liked to batter the glass—picking the weak links out from the crowd, the ones who broke under her gaze.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "harried" to describe the white museum patrons whose own anxieties make them vulnerable to Cora's stare—their harried lives are nothing compared to hers, but she uses their weakness to assert her power.
ex_zh: The novel's harried characters (the matron, the clerk, the museum visitors) are all caught up in the machinery of white supremacy, and their harried state reveals that the system does not only damage the enslaved—it also deforms the lives of those who benefit from it.

def_en: a tight grasp; a group or set
def_zh: clutch of children, clutch of eggs, clutch at straws
quote_en: The mother clutched her child's hand as they crossed the busy street.
quote_zh: A harried matron corralling an unruly clutch of children—she was one of the weak links Cora selected, the woman who would go home and never know why she felt so unsettled by a slave girl's stare.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "clutch" to describe a group of children, but also to evoke the grasping, controlling nature of white motherhood—the matron clutches her children as if they are possessions, just as the plantation owner clutches his slaves.
ex_zh: The novel's clutches (of children, of slaves, of fugitives) all involve the exercise of power, and the act of clutching is always ambiguous—it can be protective or possessive, loving or controlling.

def_en: a small, showy trinket or decoration
def_zh: pretty bauble, shiny bauble, worthless bauble
quote_en: The Christmas tree was covered with colorful baubles and tinsel.
quote_zh: Cora held the idea of freedom up to the light like a hideous bauble, tilting it so—was it real, or merely a pretty thing that would shatter when she tried to grasp it?
