ex_en: Whitehead uses "bauble" to question the reality of freedom—is it a solid thing or just a pretty bauble that will break when you try to use it? The novel's answer is ambiguous.
ex_zh: The novel's baubles (the promise of South Carolina, the museum's exhibits, the Friday night festivals) are all hollow, shiny surfaces that conceal the violence underneath, and Cora learns to see through them—but the bauble of freedom may be the only thing worth chasing.

def_en: to laugh in a noisy, gleeful way
def_zh: chortle with glee, chortle at a joke, chortle loudly
quote_en: The children chortled at the clown's silly antics.
quote_zh: "More likely he had collected last night's dregs at the saloon," and Cobb and Carpenter chortled at their prank on the young medical student—a prank that involved alcohol from a man whose health was already failing.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "chortle" to describe the grave robbers' laughter at their own cruelty, showing how the degradation of the dead becomes a source of amusement for those who profit from it.
ex_zh: The novel's chortles (Cobb and Carpenter, the white audience at the coon show, the gawkers at the auction) are all sounds of callousness, the laughter of those who have lost their capacity for empathy.

def_en: happening in the opposite way to what is expected
def_zh: ironic twist, ironic comment, ironic humor
quote_en: It was ironic that the fire station burned down while the firefighters were away at a training exercise.
quote_zh: The city had started hanging grave robbers lately—which was ironic or fitting depending on one's perspective, as the bodies of the hanged were given to medical schools for dissection, the punishment feeding the very industry it sought to destroy.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ironic" to describe the relationship between punishment and profit—the state hangs grave robbers, then gives their bodies to the medical schools, creating a closed loop of exploitation.
ex_zh: The novel's ironies (the underground railroad being literally a railroad, the "free" colored people of South Carolina being sterilized, the Freedom Trail lined with corpses) all point to the central irony of America: a nation founded on liberty that depends on slavery.

def_en: a dead body, especially one used for medical dissection
def_zh: human cadaver, cadaver dissection, cadaver lab
quote_en: Medical students studied human anatomy using cadavers donated to the university.
quote_zh: In death the negro became a human being—only then was he the white man's equal, the cadaver on the dissection table a miracle in Stevens's view, providing instruction into the intricacies of God's design.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "cadaver" to expose the terrible irony of medical science: the black body is only valuable when it is dead and can be cut up for the benefit of white students.
ex_zh: The novel's cadavers (the bodies in the medical school, the corpses on the Freedom Trail, the remains of the lynched) are the raw material of white progress—their deaths are the foundation upon which American medicine and American prosperity are built.

def_en: the act of cutting apart a body for scientific examination
def_zh: anatomical dissection, surgical dissection, dissection table
quote_en: The biology class performed a frog dissection to learn about amphibian anatomy.
quote_zh: Stevens's first anatomy course required two complete dissections—how was he supposed to pay for that? The cost of learning the body's secrets was measured not in dollars alone but in the dignity of the dead, whose consent was never sought.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dissection" to represent the violence of medical science, which treats the dead as objects to be cut apart for knowledge—a knowledge that is then used to control the living.
ex_zh: The novel's dissections (of cadavers, of the enslaved in medical experiments, of the social body by the state) all involve cutting something open to see how it works, but the thing being cut is never asked for permission, and the knowledge gained is always used to reinforce existing power structures.

def_en: to steal things of little value
def_zh: pilfer supplies, pilfer from the till, pilfer and steal
quote_en: The clerk was caught pilfering office supplies from the stockroom.
quote_zh: Leave a prospect to too late in the evening and you were liable to discover someone else had pilfered the body first—the grave-robbing trade was competitive, and the dead were a commodity like any other in America.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "pilfer" to describe the theft of corpses, showing how even the dead are not safe from the commodification that defines American life—everything, even a body, can be stolen and sold.
ex_zh: The novel's pilfering (of bodies, of food, of freedom) reveals that in a slave society, property is everything and personhood is nothing—the slave who pilfers a potato is punished, but the master who pilfers a person's life is celebrated.

def_en: a cunning plan or action designed to gain an advantage
def_zh: clever ploy, advertising ploy, ploy to distract
quote_en: The discount was just a ploy to attract customers into the store.
quote_zh: In his glory days, Carpenter elevated the ploys and chicanery of his trade to a devilish art, selling the same cadaver to multiple schools and even reporting his own stolen bodies to the police for a second fee.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ploy" to describe the grave robbers' schemes, showing that criminal ingenuity can be as sophisticated as any legal enterprise—the ploy is the same whether you are selling cadavers or cotton.
ex_zh: The novel's ploys (Carpenter's schemes, Ridgeway's traps, the underground railroad's deceptions) all involve trickery, and the line between the criminal and the heroic ploy is drawn not by method but by purpose—one ploy serves the slave system, the other serves the fugitive.

def_en: the use of trickery or deception to achieve a purpose
def_zh: legal chicanery, political chicanery, chicanery and fraud
quote_en: The election was marred by accusations of chicanery and voter fraud.
quote_zh: Midnight meetings, riverbank chicanery, intrigue at the crossroads—Donald's many work trips during Martin's childhood were in fact abolitionist missions, and his son would inherit both the map and the danger.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "chicanery" to describe the methods of the underground railroad, which are necessarily secretive and deceptive because the open pursuit of freedom is illegal.
ex_zh: The novel's chicanery (forged passes, hidden tunnels, coded messages) is the language of resistance—in a slave society, honesty is a luxury that the enslaved cannot afford, and chicanery becomes a virtue.

def_en: a period of keeping awake during the night to watch or pray
def_zh: keep vigil, night vigil, candlelight vigil
quote_en: The family kept a vigil at their son's bedside while he remained in a coma.
quote_zh: Eventually the body trade grew so reckless that relatives took to holding graveside vigils, lest their loved ones disappear in the night—each vigil a testament to the desperation of those who could not even trust the dead to rest.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "vigil" to show how the slave system violates even the sanctity of death—relatives must guard the graves of their loved ones to prevent them from being stolen and dissected.
ex_zh: The novel's vigils (at gravesides, in attics, on the underground railroad) are all acts of desperate watchfulness, the constant alertness of those who can never rest because the world is always trying to take something from them.

def_en: having a skin disease that causes hair loss; shabby or squalid
def_zh: mangy dog, mangy coat, mangy appearance
quote_en: The mangy stray dog limped down the alley, looking for scraps.
quote_zh: Carpenter snarled when he said the word, a mangy dog hoarding his bone: nigger. Stevens never used the word, disapproving of racial prejudice—yet he benefited from it, cutting into black bodies that no white family would claim.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "mangy" to describe Carpenter, whose own degraded state mirrors the degradation he inflicts on the bodies he steals—the grave robber is a mangy creature, but he is also a product of the same system that produces mangy slaves.
ex_zh: The novel's mangy characters (Carpenter, the poor whites, the louts) are all at the bottom of the white social hierarchy, but they are still above the black population, and their mangy status does not prevent them from participating in the exploitation of those even lower than themselves.

def_en: to burn slowly with smoke but no flame
def_zh: smoulder with anger, smoulder in the ashes, smoulder and burn
quote_en: The campfire smouldered for hours after the campers left.
quote_zh: She had no idea how long the fire had been smouldering before it caught, but by the time the family roused from their sleep, the house was already lost—a warning to Martin about what happened to those who harbored fugitives.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "smoulder" to describe the hidden danger of the underground railroad—the fire of resistance smoulders beneath the surface, and when it finally catches, it destroys everything in its path.
ex_zh: The novel's smouldering fires (the burning house, the wildfire in Tennessee, the anger in Cora's heart) all represent the slow accumulation of heat that eventually explodes into violence, reminding us that the slave system cannot contain the rage it creates.

def_en: the burial of a dead body
def_zh: proper interment, interment ceremony, interment site
quote_en: The soldier's interment at Arlington National Cemetery was attended by hundreds of mourners.
quote_zh: Cora crawled back up the steps, perching just beneath the door, and listened—it might be day or night in the world above, and her interment in this underground station might last hours or weeks, depending on whether anyone remembered she was there.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "interment" to describe Cora's time in the abandoned station, where she is buried alive—a living interment that mirrors the death of those who never made it out of the tunnel.
ex_zh: The novel's interments (of the dead, of the living in tunnels and attics, of the memory of the enslaved) all involve being placed underground and forgotten, but the underground railroad transforms interment from a burial into a passage—the tunnel is a grave that becomes a womb.

def_en: rough and harsh-sounding
def_zh: raspy voice, raspy cough, raspy whisper
quote_en: The old man's raspy voice cracked as he tried to sing the national anthem.
quote_zh: She almost keeled over into the tracks as she howled after the train, her throat raspy and raw after days of privation—a sound of pure despair that no human should have to make, and no train should have ignored.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "raspy" to describe Cora's voice after she has been abandoned in the tunnel—her raspy howl is the sound of a person pushed beyond endurance, a sound that is almost inhuman because it comes from a place deeper than language.
ex_zh: The novel's raspy voices (Cora's, the engineer's, the dying) are all sounds of bodies pushed to their limits, and the raspiness is a reminder that the underground railroad is not a romantic adventure but a desperate struggle where the body is constantly breaking down.

def_en: a state of lacking basic necessities; hardship
def_zh: suffer privation, economic privation, wartime privation
quote_en: The prisoners endured months of privation, with little food and no heat.
quote_zh: She had starved before, when Connelly got it in his mind to punish the quarter and cut off rations, but the cotton demanded the punishment be brief—here, there was no way to know when she would eat next, a privation without end or purpose.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "privation" to describe the condition of the fugitive, who is always hungry, always cold, always at the edge of death—privation is not an interruption of life but the texture of it.
ex_zh: The novel's privations (hunger, cold, exhaustion) are the daily reality of the enslaved, and the fugitive's flight does not end privation but only changes its shape—on the run, you are still hungry, but at least the hunger is your own.

def_en: to delay or linger; to stay longer than intended
def_zh: tarry too long, tarry at the inn, tarry and wait
quote_en: We cannot tarry any longer if we want to catch the last train.
quote_zh: May the next traveler not tarry and keep moving up the line, all the way to liberty—Cora's prayer for whoever might next find themselves in the darkness of the abandoned station, waiting for a train that might never come.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "tarry" to warn against the temptation to linger in any station, any refuge, any moment of safety—to tarry is to risk capture, and the fugitive must always be moving.
ex_zh: The novel's warnings against tarrying (Cora's decision to stay in South Carolina, the Valentine farm's debate about closing its doors, the temptation to settle) suggest that in a slave society, no place is safe for long, and the only way to survive is to keep moving.

def_en: tender, juicy, and tasty
def_zh: succulent meat, succulent fruit, succulent and flavorful
quote_en: The roasted chicken was succulent and flavorful, cooked to perfection.
quote_zh: The breeze tickled her skin and she gulped the air like water, the night sky the best meal she had ever had, the stars made succulent and ripe after her time below—freedom tasted like nothing she had imagined, and everything she had hoped.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "succulent" to describe the taste of freedom, which is not a flavor but an experience—the air itself becomes succulent when you have been deprived of it for so long.
ex_zh: The novel's succulent moments (Cora emerging from the tunnel, the taste of clean water, the first breath of fresh air) are all sensations of release, and the word "succulent" captures the physical pleasure of being alive after you have been buried in darkness.

def_en: to reveal or show the presence of a quality or feeling
def_zh: evince interest, evince concern, evince emotion
quote_en: The painting evinces a deep understanding of human suffering.
quote_zh: For an agent of the underground railroad, presumably no stranger to peril and risk, Martin evinced a nervous personality, his round red face poking through the rubble as if he were the one who needed rescuing rather than the fugitive before him.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "evince" to describe Martin's demeanor, which reveals that the agents of the underground railroad are not superheroes but ordinary, fearful people whose courage is all the more remarkable because they are afraid.
ex_zh: The novel's evincing of fear (in Martin, in Sam, in Cora) shows that bravery is not the absence of fear but the willingness to act despite it—the underground railroad is built by people who evince terror and still do the work.

def_en: moderate in eating and drinking; temperate
def_zh: abstemious diet, abstemious lifestyle, abstemious habits
quote_en: The monk led an abstemious life, eating only one small meal per day.
quote_zh: Judge Tennyson was a respected figure in town when abstemious, but this night he tottered—the man who would sentence Martin and Ethel to the hanging tree could barely stand, his judgment as clouded as his vision.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "abstemious" to describe the judge's sober condition, which is the only time he is respected—when he drinks, his moral corruption becomes visible, but the system still allows him to judge others.
ex_zh: The novel's abstemious figures (the judge when sober, the temperance advocates, the respectable citizens) are not necessarily more moral than the drunkards—their abstemiousness is a mask that covers the same cruelty, just better hidden.

def_en: a false, absurd, or distorted representation of something
def_zh: travesty of justice, travesty of truth, travesty and mockery
quote_en: The trial was a travesty of justice, with the verdict decided before it began.
quote_zh: She'd heard of coon shows but had never witnessed their travesties; the white men in burned cork capered through skits that mocked the very humanity of the people they pretended to portray.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "travesty" to describe the coon shows, which are a travesty not only of black people but of the very idea of representation—they show how the slave system turns human beings into caricatures for white amusement.
ex_zh: The novel's travesties (the coon shows, the museum exhibits, the Friday night lynchings) are all performances that distort the truth of slavery for an audience that wants to be entertained rather than confronted, and the travesty is that the audience believes what they see.

def_en: difficult to control or predict because of willful or perverse behavior
def_zh: wayward child, wayward tendencies, wayward path
quote_en: The wayward teenager refused to follow any of the house rules.
quote_zh: The wayward path of the fugitives through swamps and forests mirrored the chaos of a nation that could not decide whether to embrace freedom or tighten the chains of bondage.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wayward" to describe both the fugitives' route and the nation's moral trajectory—neither follows a straight line, and both are marked by false turns and unexpected detours.
ex_zh: The novel's waywardness (Cora's journey, the underground railroad's routes, the plot itself) suggests that there is no clear path to freedom, only a wayward groping toward the light, and that the wayward path is the only path there is.

def_en: lying face down on the ground in submission or exhaustion; to weaken or overcome
def_zh: prostrate before the king, prostrate with grief, prostrate body
quote_en: The heat wave left the workers prostrate on the factory floor.
quote_zh: Cora found herself prostrate not only from Ridgeway's beatings but from the weight of every generation of her family who had been ground into the soil of the Randall plantation.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "prostrate" to describe Cora's physical and spiritual exhaustion—she is prostrate under the weight of history, her body bearing the accumulated trauma of her ancestors.
ex_zh: The novel's prostrate bodies (Cora after the whippings, the slaves in the fields, the victims of the lynchings) are all evidence of the violence that keeps the system running, but they are also positions of vulnerability that can become positions of resistance—the prostrate body can rise.

def_en: the occasion of a person's birth; the place of origin
def_zh: nativity scene, nativity story, place of nativity
quote_en: The museum displayed a painting of the nativity scene from the 15th century.
quote_zh: The nativity of the American nation was stained by the contradiction between its declaration of liberty and its dependence on slavery—a wound that would fester for centuries.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "nativity" to refer to the founding of America, which is a birth stained by original sin—the nation's nativity is not a moment of pure promise but a moment of hypocrisy that continues to poison the present.
ex_zh: The novel's treatment of nativity (Ajarry's origins in Africa, Cora's birth on the plantation, the nation's founding) suggests that where you come from determines where you can go, and that the stain of slavery is on every American nativity.

def_en: not able to be maintained or defended against attack
def_zh: untenable position, untenable argument, untenable situation
quote_en: After the scandal, his position as director became untenable.
quote_zh: The moral position of the slaveholding class was untenable, yet they clung to it with violence and terror, lynching any who dared to point out the obvious hypocrisy.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "untenable" to describe the slaveholders' ideology, which is logically and morally untenable but is maintained through force—the untenable becomes tenable when backed by violence.
ex_zh: The novel's untenable situations (the slave's position, the fugitive's hiding place, the underground railroad's stations) are all made untenable by the slave system, but the system itself is the most untenable of all—yet it persists, because those who benefit from it refuse to let it go.

def_en: a person who deserts one party or cause to join an opposing one
def_zh: turncoat traitor, political turncoat, turncoat and betrayer
quote_en: The general was branded a turncoat after switching sides during the war.
quote_zh: On the plantation, a turncoat slave who informed on fellow fugitives could buy temporary safety, but the masters never truly trusted those who had betrayed their own kind.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "turncoat" to describe informers, whose betrayal is both understandable (they are trying to survive) and unforgivable (they condemn others to death).
ex_zh: The novel's turncoats (the slaves who inform, the white sympathizers who betray, the politicians who switch sides) all face the same dilemma: in a slave society, loyalty is impossible because the system is designed to turn everyone against everyone else.

def_en: to reject or void, especially by legal procedure; to suppress forcefully
def_zh: quash a rebellion, quash a rumor, quash a subpoena
quote_en: The court moved to quash the subpoena, citing lack of evidence.
quote_zh: The white militias were called upon to quash every whisper of rebellion, but the more violently they suppressed, the deeper the desire for freedom grew in the hearts of the enslaved.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "quash" to describe the state's response to resistance—every attempt to quash rebellion only plants the seeds of the next one, because the desire for freedom cannot be quashed by violence.
ex_zh: The novel's quashed rebellions (the slave revolts, the underground railroad, the Valentine farm) are all temporarily suppressed, but they are never truly quashed—the spirit of resistance survives the destruction of its visible forms.

def_en: inciting or causing people to rebel against lawful authority
def_zh: seditious speech, seditious literature, seditious conspiracy
quote_en: The government banned the pamphlet as seditious material.
quote_zh: Teaching a slave to read was considered a seditious act in most southern states, for literacy was the key that unlocked not only letters but the very idea that bondage could be escaped.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "seditious" to describe acts that are criminalized precisely because they threaten the foundations of the slave system—literacy is seditious because it gives the enslaved the tools to imagine freedom.
ex_zh: The novel's seditious acts (reading, running, hiding) are all treated as crimes by the state, but the novel itself is seditious, because it asks readers to imagine a world without slavery and to recognize that the laws protecting slavery are not just.

def_en: the quality of behaving with prudence and restraint; the freedom to decide
def_zh: judicial discretion, professional discretion, discretion advised
quote_en: The manager used her discretion to approve the refund without paperwork.
quote_zh: The punishment for aiding a fugitive was left to the discretion of local authorities in North Carolina, which in practice meant death—for discretion in the hands of the mob was merely another word for vengeance.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "discretion" to expose the lie of local control—when punishment is left to local discretion, it means that the most violent elements of the community can do whatever they want.
ex_zh: The novel's discretion (the judge's, the patrollers', the mob's) is always exercised against the colored population, and the word "discretion" becomes a mask for arbitrary violence—there is no discretion when the outcome is predetermined.

def_en: a long-standing rival or archenemy; an agent of retribution
def_zh: arch-nemesis, nemesis of justice, meet one's nemesis
quote_en: The detective finally confronted his nemesis in a dark alley.
quote_zh: Ridgeway became Cora's nemesis not because he was the most brutal slave catcher, but because he made the hunt personal—her family's escape was a flaw in the American imperative that only her recapture could repair.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "nemesis" to describe the relationship between Ridgeway and Cora, which is not just that of hunter and prey but of two people whose fates are intertwined—each is the other's nemesis, and their struggle defines them both.
ex_zh: The novel's nemeses (Ridgeway and Mabel, Ridgeway and Cora, the slave system and the fugitive) are locked in a dance that neither can escape—the nemesis is the mirror that shows you who you really are.

def_en: a cause of great trouble or suffering
def_zh: trials and tribulations, tribulation and hardship, endure tribulation
quote_en: The family endured many tribulations during the long winter without heat.
quote_zh: The tribulations of the underground railroad were etched into every mile of track laid in darkness, every fugitive who died within sight of freedom, every station master who swung from a tree.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "tribulation" to name the suffering that is inseparable from the struggle for freedom—there is no escape from tribulation, only the choice of which tribulation to endure.
ex_zh: The novel's tribulations (the whippings, the lynchings, the medical experiments) are the price that the enslaved pay for the chance of freedom, and the underground railroad is a machine for transforming tribulation into hope.

def_en: to drink; to absorb or assimilate ideas or knowledge
def_zh: imbibe alcohol, imbibe knowledge, imbibe the culture
quote_en: The philosopher imbibed the teachings of the ancient Greeks during his years abroad.
quote_zh: Caesar had imbibed from Mrs. Garner not only letters and reading but the dangerous belief that freedom was his birthright—a belief that would cost him his life on the Randall plantation.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "imbibe" to describe the absorption of ideas that are forbidden in the slave system—imbibing the belief in freedom is like drinking poison, because it makes you unable to accept your bondage.
ex_zh: The novel's imbibing (of literacy, of abolitionist ideas, of hope) is always dangerous, because the slave system punishes those who have imbibed the wrong beliefs—but it is also the only path to liberation, because you cannot run if you do not believe you can.

def_en: to jump or dance around excitedly
def_zh: cavort and dance, cavort with joy, cavort in the field
quote_en: The children cavorted in the sprinklers on the hot summer day.
quote_zh: The slaves were forced to cavort for the amusement of the Randall brothers, their pretended joy a mask for the terror that tightened around their hearts with every forced smile.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "cavort" to expose the coerced performance of happiness that is demanded of the enslaved—their cavorting is not joy but a survival strategy, a way of hiding their true feelings from the masters.
ex_zh: The novel's cavorting (at Jockey's birthday, at the Friday night festivals, on the plantation) is always ambiguous—is the dancer happy or terrified? The novel refuses to answer, because the two are inseparable in the slave's experience.

def_en: an annual calendar containing weather forecasts, astronomical data, and other information
def_zh: farmer's almanac, weather almanac, old almanac
quote_en: The farmer consulted his almanac to determine the best time for planting.
quote_zh: Cora found solace in Donald's old almanacs during her months in the attic, for the tides and moon phases proceeded without regard for white men's laws—a natural order that slavery could never entirely corrupt.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "almanac" to symbolize the natural world, which operates according to its own laws and offers a refuge from the arbitrary violence of the slave system—the moon rises whether the master wills it or not.
ex_zh: The novel's almanacs (Donald's, the one Royal gives Cora, the tables of tides and stars) represent a different kind of knowledge, one that is not owned by the white man and cannot be taken away—the almanac is a map of a world that slavery cannot touch.

def_en: deep, thoughtful reflection; the act of chewing cud
def_zh: deep rumination, rumination on the past, philosophical rumination
quote_en: After hours of rumination, he finally arrived at a solution to the problem.
quote_zh: Cora's rumination on her mother's abandonment yielded no comfort, only the bitter realization that freedom sometimes required the most unforgivable betrayals.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "rumination" to describe Cora's obsessive thinking about her mother, which is both a way of processing trauma and a trap that keeps her stuck in the past—rumination can be a form of captivity as well as a form of understanding.
ex_zh: The novel's ruminations (Cora's, Mabel's, the reader's) are all attempts to make sense of suffering, but the novel suggests that some suffering cannot be understood, only endured—rumination may be a necessary part of survival, but it is also a kind of wound that never fully heals.

def_en: painful or laborious effort; the pain of childbirth
def_zh: hard travail, travail and suffering, travail of labor
quote_en: The monument honored the travail of the workers who built the railroad.
quote_zh: The travail of the enslaved was not only in the cotton fields but in the endless reproduction of bodies for the market—each birth a profit, each death a line item on a ledger.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "travail" to name both the labor of the enslaved and the pain of childbirth, linking the two forms of suffering that are the foundation of the slave system—the travail of the field and the travail of the womb are both forms of extraction.
ex_zh: The novel's travail (the cotton picking, the breeding, the escape) is the raw material of the slave economy, and the word "travail" reminds us that the wealth of America was built on the pain of black bodies—a pain that continues to travail the nation.

def_en: the time of year when the sun is at its furthest point from the equator
def_zh: summer solstice, winter solstice, solstice celebration
quote_en: The ancient monument aligned perfectly with the rising sun on the summer solstice.
quote_zh: On the winter solstice, the darkest night of the year, the fugitives huddled in the attic, knowing that each hour of darkness brought them closer to either capture or the thin, uncertain light of dawn.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "solstice" to mark the turning points in the fugitives' journey—the solstice is a moment of extreme darkness or extreme light, and the fugitives are always at the edge, never in the middle.
ex_zh: The novel's solstices (the longest night in the attic, the brightest day in South Carolina) are moments of transition, but the novel suggests that for the fugitive, every day is a solstice—an extreme of either hope or despair, with no temperate zone in between.

def_en: involvement in a wrongful act or crime
def_zh: complicity in crime, silent complicity, complicity and guilt
quote_en: The guard's complicity in the escape was discovered after the investigation.
quote_zh: Ethel's complicity in harboring Cora was not born of abolitionist conviction but of a twisted longing to possess a savage of her own—a complicity that would end with her tied to the hanging tree.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "complicity" to describe the many forms of involvement in the slave system—from the masters to the patrollers to the ordinary citizens who do nothing, everyone is complicit, and complicity has many motives, some of them strange and self-destructive.
ex_zh: The novel's complicity (Ethel's, the gawkers', the readers') forces us to ask: where do we stand? Are we complicit in the violence we read about, or does our reading make us witnesses? The novel offers no easy answer.

def_en: shriveled or wrinkled with age
def_zh: wizened old man, wizened face, wizened and bent
quote_en: The wizened old woman had lived alone in the mountain cabin for fifty years.
quote_zh: The wizened face of Jockey, the oldest slave on the Randall plantation, told a story that no history book could capture—a century of survival through whips, chains, and the daily negation of his humanity.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wizened" to describe Jockey's appearance, which is a map of his suffering—every wrinkle is a scar, every line a year of survival, and his wizened face is a rebuke to the system that tried to destroy him.
ex_zh: The novel's wizened characters (Jockey, Old Abraham, the Hob women) are all survivors, and their wizened bodies are evidence that the slave system does not always win—sometimes, against all odds, the body endures, and endurance is a form of victory.

def_en: refusal to change one's views or to agree
def_zh: political intransigence, intransigence and inflexibility, stubborn intransigence
quote_en: The intransigence of both sides made the peace negotiations impossible.
quote_zh: The intransigence of the slaveholding class, their refusal to countenance any alternative to bondage, made civil war inevitable—and ensured that the underground railroad would run on blood as much as coal.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "intransigence" to describe the slaveholders' unwillingness to compromise, which doomed the nation to war—their intransigence was both the cause of the war and the justification for the underground railroad's existence.
ex_zh: The novel's intransigence (the masters', the fugitives', the abolitionists') is a double-edged sword—it can be a vice when it prevents compromise, but it can also be a virtue when it refuses to accept the unacceptable, and the underground railroad is built on the intransigence of those who refuse to accept slavery.

def_en: the omission of words from a sentence; a set of dots indicating such an omission
def_zh: ellipsis points, ellipsis in grammar, use of ellipsis
quote_en: The author used ellipsis to suggest the character's unspoken thoughts.
quote_zh: Mabel's escape ended not with a triumphant arrival in the North but with an ellipsis—a body swallowed by the swamp, a story that Cora would spend her whole life trying to complete.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ellipsis" as a structural device: the novel leaves gaps, omissions, things unsaid—Mabel's fate is an ellipsis, and the reader must fill in the missing words with imagination and grief.
ex_zh: The novel's ellipses (Mabel's disappearance, Lovey's fate, the gaps between sections) are invitations to the reader to complete the story, but they are also reminders that some stories cannot be fully told—the ellipsis is the shape of trauma, a story that cannot be spoken.

def_en: fine, feathery, or faint
def_zh: wispy clouds, wispy hair, wispy trail
quote_en: Wispy clouds drifted across the pale blue sky.
quote_zh: The wispy remains of the slave quarters, barely visible through the morning mist, were all that was left of a generation of human beings who had lived, loved, and died without ever being called by their true names.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wispy" to describe the traces of the enslaved that remain after they are gone—their presence is wispy, barely visible, easily overlooked, but it is still there for those who know how to look.
ex_zh: The novel's wispy traces (the abandoned cabins, the overgrown graves, the faded memories) are all that is left of millions of lives, and the wispy quality of these traces is a reproach to a nation that has tried to erase them—they are wispy, but they are not gone.

def_en: a cause of great suffering; a whip used for punishment
def_zh: scourge of war, scourge of disease, scourge and torment
quote_en: Poverty was the scourge of the industrial city, claiming thousands each winter.
quote_zh: The scourge of slavery was not merely the lash on the back but the systematic erasure of memory, language, and kinship—a violence that continued long after the chains were removed.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "scourge" to name slavery itself, which is a scourge not only in the sense of a whip but in the sense of a plague—slavery is a disease that infects the entire society, and its effects cannot be cured by simply removing the chains.
ex_zh: The novel's scourges (the whip, the plantation, the medical experiments) are all instruments of torture, but the greatest scourge is the loss of memory—the forgetting of the enslaved, the erasure of their names, the transformation of their suffering into statistics.

def_en: a violation or breach of a law or rule
def_zh: minor infraction, traffic infraction, infraction of rules
quote_en: The player received a penalty for a minor infraction of the rules.
quote_zh: On the plantation, the smallest infraction—looking a white man in the eye, learning a letter, hesitating for a moment too long—could result in a whipping that left the back permanently scarred.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "infraction" to show how the slave system criminalizes normal human behavior—every action of the enslaved is potentially an infraction, because the law is designed to find fault with anything they do.
ex_zh: The novel's infractions (reading, running, looking) are all acts that would be innocent in a free society, but in the slave system they are capital crimes—the infraction is not in the act but in the existence of the actor, and the only way to avoid infraction is to be invisible.

def_en: a brief evocative scene or description
def_zh: short vignette, vignette of life, literary vignette
quote_en: The book opens with a vignette of the protagonist's childhood in rural France.
quote_zh: Each chapter of The Underground Railroad is a vignette of a different state, each with its own method of racial control—from South Carolina's medical experiments to North Carolina's public lynchings.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "vignette" to describe the novel's structure, which is not a continuous narrative but a series of brief, intense scenes that together create a mosaic of American slavery.
ex_zh: The novel's vignettes (Georgia, South Carolina, North Carolina, Tennessee, Indiana) are like a museum diorama—each one is complete in itself, but together they tell a larger story, and the gaps between them are as important as the scenes themselves.

def_en: a plot or scheme, especially one that is devious
def_zh: political machinations, machinations of power, behind-the-scenes machinations
quote_en: The novel exposes the political machinations behind the corporate takeover.
quote_zh: The machinations of the slave catchers, from bribed informants to elaborate traps, were ultimately powerless against the simple, unquenchable human desire to be free.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "machination" to describe the complex systems of control that the slaveholders devise, but the novel suggests that all their machinations cannot extinguish the human spirit—the machinations are elaborate, but freedom is simple.
ex_zh: The novel's machinations (the underground railroad's codes, Ridgeway's strategies, the medical experiments) are all attempts to control what cannot be controlled, and the failure of machination is the novel's central hope—no matter how cunning the oppressor, the desire for freedom will find a way.

def_en: pale and giving the appearance of illness or exhaustion
def_zh: wan smile, wan complexion, wan and weary
quote_en: She gave a wan smile after three sleepless nights in the hospital.
quote_zh: The faces of the Hob women were wan and hollow-eyed, their bodies broken not only by labor but by the knowledge that the plantation had stolen everything from them except the slow, grinding misery of survival.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wan" to describe the appearance of those who have been broken by the system—their wanness is the physical evidence of the violence done to them, a violence that leaves the body but does not kill it, because death would be a mercy.
ex_zh: The novel's wan characters (the Hob women, the victims of the medical experiments, the survivors of the lynchings) are all still alive, but their wanness is a kind of death-in-life, and the novel asks whether survival is enough when survival means being wan and hollow-eyed until the end.

def_en: a clumsy, stupid person
def_zh: big lummox, clumsy lummox, lummox and oaf
quote_en: The lummox tripped over his own feet and spilled coffee on the client.
quote_zh: Fiona called the night rider a lummox when he failed to discover Cora in the attic, but the real foolishness belonged to those who believed they could build a nation on slavery without eventually being consumed by its cruelty.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lummox" as a term of abuse that Fiona hurls at the patroller, but the novel turns the insult back on the entire slave system—the real lummox is the system itself, which is clumsy, stupid, and ultimately self-destructive.
ex_zh: The novel's lummoxes (the night riders, the patrollers, the masters) are all agents of a system that is fundamentally stupid—it cannot see that it is digging its own grave, and the lummox who fails to find Cora in the attic is a symbol of the system's inevitable failure.

def_en: a person sent to promote religion in a foreign country
def_zh: Christian missionary, missionary work, missionary zeal
quote_en: The missionary spent thirty years living among the tribe he sought to convert.
quote_zh: Ethel dreamed of being a missionary in Africa, never realizing that the savage she sought to civilize would arrive at her own door—a fugitive girl whose humanity would finally shatter her fantasies of racial superiority.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "missionary" to critique the colonial mindset that sees non-white peoples as savages in need of civilizing—Ethel's missionary fantasies are a form of racism, and her encounter with Cora reveals the lie at the heart of the missionary project.
ex_zh: The novel's missionaries (Ethel, the white reformers in South Carolina, the abolitionists with their own agendas) are all trying to save the colored population according to their own terms, but the novel suggests that true liberation cannot be given by missionaries—it must be taken by the fugitives themselves.

def_en: deceitful; acting in bad faith
def_zh: duplicitous behavior, duplicitous politician, duplicitous scheme
quote_en: The duplicitous politician promised reform while taking bribes from lobbyists.
quote_zh: The slave catchers' duplicitous methods—posing as abolitionists, bribing informants, forging documents—revealed that the moral corruption of slavery infected not only the masters but all who served the institution.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "duplicitous" to describe the methods of the slave catchers, who use deception as a weapon—their duplicity is a mirror of the duplicity of the slave system itself, which pretends to be benevolent while committing atrocities.
ex_zh: The novel's duplicitous characters (the slave catchers, the doctors, the white sympathizers who betray) all show that in a slave society, trust is impossible—everyone is potentially duplicitous, and the only defense is to trust no one.

def_en: a representative sent on a mission
def_zh: emissary of peace, royal emissary, emissary and envoy
quote_en: The king sent an emissary to negotiate the terms of peace.
quote_zh: Valentine farm became an emissary of hope for fugitives throughout the region, its very existence a message that a community of free black people could thrive—until the white mobs proved that no emissary of liberation would be tolerated.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "emissary" to describe the Valentine farm, which sends a message of hope to fugitives everywhere—the farm is an emissary of possibility, but the mobs that destroy it are also emissaries, carrying the message that black freedom will not be allowed.
ex_zh: The novel's emissaries (the underground railroad agents, the fugitives themselves, the survivors of the massacre) are all carrying a message that the system wants to suppress, and the novel is itself an emissary, carrying the story of slavery to readers who must decide what to do with it.

def_en: to free someone from a mistaken belief
def_zh: disabuse of the notion, disabuse of ignorance, disabuse a misconception
quote_en: She tried to disabuse him of the notion that wealth would bring happiness.
quote_zh: The brutal reality of the Randall plantation quickly disabused Caesar of the mild slavery he had known in Virginia, replacing his memories of Mrs. Garner's kindness with the lash and the auction block.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "disabuse" to describe the process by which the enslaved learn the truth about the slave system—Caesar's Virginia experience was a lie, and the Randall plantation disabuses him of that lie, replacing it with a harsher truth.
ex_zh: The novel's disabusing (of Caesar, of Cora, of the reader) is a painful process—the novel disabuses us of any romantic notions about slavery, showing us the whip, the chain, the auction block, and the medical experiments, and forcing us to see the truth we would rather ignore.

def_en: influence or power, especially in politics or business
def_zh: political clout, economic clout, clout and influence
quote_en: The former senator still has considerable clout in Washington.
quote_zh: In the slaveholding South, a master's clout was measured not only in acres of cotton but in the number of human beings he could buy, sell, and destroy without ever facing a judge or a conscience.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "clout" to describe the power of the slaveholder, which is absolute and unchecked—the master's clout extends over the bodies and souls of everyone on his plantation, and there is no appeal from his decisions.
ex_zh: The novel's clout (the masters', the slave catchers', the state's) is the central fact of the slave system—clout is the ability to do violence without consequence, and the novel shows that clout corrupts absolutely, turning men into monsters and women into victims.

def_en: a storehouse for threshed grain
def_zh: grain granary, community granary, granary and silo
quote_en: The village granary was filled with enough wheat to last through the winter.
quote_zh: The fertile lands of the American South became a granary for the industrial North, but the grain was watered by the sweat and blood of enslaved men, women, and children who never tasted the bread they grew.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "granary" to expose the economic relationship between the slave South and the industrial North—the South is a granary, but the granary is built on the labor of the enslaved, and the North profits from that labor while pretending to be morally superior.
ex_zh: The novel's granaries (the cotton fields, the rice plantations, the corn fields) are all sites of extraction, where the labor of the enslaved is harvested and shipped north, and the novel suggests that the entire American economy is a granary built on the backs of black people.

def_en: to stare in an angry or sullen way
def_zh: glower at, glower with anger, glower and scowl
quote_en: The teacher glowered at the student who had been talking during the lesson.
quote_zh: Ridgeway's glower could silence a room, but Cora learned to meet his gaze without flinching—a small rebellion that reminded him that even in chains, a slave's spirit could not be entirely broken.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "glower" to describe Ridgeway's intimidating stare, which is a weapon of psychological control—but Cora's refusal to be cowed by his glower is an act of resistance, a way of saying that she sees him and is not afraid.
ex_zh: The novel's glowers (Ridgeway's, Terrance's, the patrollers') are all attempts to dominate through the eyes, but the novel shows that the glower loses its power when the person being glowered at refuses to look away—the gaze can be a weapon, but it can also be a shield.

def_en: to strip of covering or possessions
def_zh: denude the land, denude of vegetation, denude of dignity
quote_en: The logging operation denuded the hillside of all vegetation.
quote_zh: The white settlers denuded the land of its forests and its original inhabitants, clearing the way for cotton—a crop that would in turn denude the bodies of the enslaved who worked it.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "denude" to describe the double violence of colonialism: the land is denuded of its forests and its people, and then the bodies of the enslaved are denuded of their dignity, their names, their families—denudation is the signature of the American project.
ex_zh: The novel's denuded landscapes (the burned forests of Tennessee, the cleared fields of Georgia, the empty spaces where villages once stood) are the physical evidence of the violence that made America, and the denuded bodies of the enslaved are the human evidence—both are stripped bare, and both are left to suffer.

def_en: to inspire or permeate with a feeling or quality
def_zh: imbue with meaning, imbue with spirit, imbue with hope
quote_en: The mentor sought to imbue his students with a love of learning.
quote_zh: The underground railroad imbued the word "freedom" with a meaning far beyond the dictionary—it meant risking death, betraying family, and walking hundreds of miles through swamps with nothing but a handful of yams and a star to guide you.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "imbue" to describe how the underground railroad transforms abstract concepts into lived experience—freedom is not just a word; it is something that the fugitives imbue with their sweat, their blood, their terror, and their hope.
ex_zh: The novel's imbuing (of freedom, of hope, of the underground railroad) is the central act of the narrative—the novel takes the historical facts of slavery and imbues them with the emotional weight of individual experience, making us feel what the fugitives felt, and in doing so, it imbues us with the responsibility to remember.

def_en: very attentive to detail; fussy
def_zh: fastidious about cleanliness, fastidious dresser, fastidious and meticulous
quote_en: The fastidious editor caught every typo in the manuscript.
quote_zh: Homer was fastidious about his black suit and stovepipe hat, extracting lint from the fabric as if it were a poison spider—a strange precision in a boy who helped his master chain human beings for a living.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "fastidious" to describe Homer's attention to his appearance, which is a form of dignity in a boy who has no other dignity—his fastidiousness is a way of asserting control over his own body when everything else is controlled by Ridgeway.
ex_zh: The novel's fastidious characters (Homer, the doctors, the museum curator) are all concerned with details, but the novel asks whether fastidiousness is a form of resistance (Homer) or a form of cruelty (the doctors)—the answer depends on who is being fastidious and why.

def_en: to bully or harass
def_zh: hector and intimidate, hector the staff, hector into submission
quote_en: The older boys would hector the newcomers until they cried.
quote_zh: The overseer hectored the field hands constantly, his whip always within reach, but the slaves learned that the true power of the oppressor lies not in the lash but in the victim's belief that resistance is impossible.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "hector" to describe the constant harassment that is the texture of plantation life—the hectoring is designed to break the spirit, to make the enslaved believe that resistance is futile, but the novel shows that hectoring only works if the victim accepts it.
ex_zh: The novel's hectoring (the overseers, the patrollers, the masters) is the daily language of the slave system, but the novel's fugitives learn to resist hectoring not by fighting back but by refusing to internalize it—they hear the hectoring, but they do not believe it.

def_en: various; of different kinds
def_zh: sundry items, sundry expenses, sundry and diverse
quote_en: The general store sold sundry items from buttons to horse harnesses.
quote_zh: The station master's duties included sundry tasks—hiding fugitives, forging passes, bribing officials, and digging graves for those who did not make it—each task a small thread in the vast tapestry of the underground railroad.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "sundry" to describe the miscellaneous, often overlooked tasks that keep the underground railroad running—the sundry duties are not glamorous, but they are essential, and they are performed by ordinary people who never appear in the history books.
ex_zh: The novel's sundry characters (the station agents, the cooks, the drivers) are the unsung heroes of the underground railroad, and the sundry tasks they perform are the small, daily acts of resistance that together make liberation possible—the sundry is the foundation of the spectacular.

def_en: showing great attention to detail
def_zh: meticulous planning, meticulous attention, meticulous and thorough
quote_en: The jeweler's meticulous workmanship made each piece unique.
quote_zh: The underground railroad required meticulous planning—safe houses spaced exactly one night's journey apart, signals that could be read only by the initiated, and codes buried in newspaper classifieds that could mean life or death.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "meticulous" to describe the planning that goes into every escape—the underground railroad is not a spontaneous uprising but a carefully organized network, and its success depends on the meticulous attention of its agents.
ex_zh: The novel's meticulous details (the coded messages, the timing of departures, the selection of safe houses) are the machinery of liberation, and the novel itself is meticulous in its research and its construction, showing that freedom is not won by accident but by painstaking, meticulous work.

def_en: to hit repeatedly; to defeat decisively
def_zh: clobber the opponent, clobber with a club, get clobbered
quote_en: The boxer clobbered his opponent in the first round.
quote_zh: Boseman clobbered the Indian scout with a shovel to win his necklace of ears, but Ridgeway knew that brute force alone could not catch Mabel's daughter—she required a hunt that was as much psychological as physical.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "clobber" to describe the brute violence that is one tool of the slave catcher—clobbering is effective in the moment, but it is not enough to capture someone like Cora, who requires a more sophisticated kind of hunting.
ex_zh: The novel's clobbering (Boseman's violence, the patrollers' beatings, the mob's lynchings) is the crude, obvious violence of the slave system, but the novel suggests that the more insidious violence is the psychological kind—the clobbering is loud, but the quiet violence of the medical experiments and the sterilization programs is just as deadly.

def_en: having a feeling of vague or regretful longing
def_zh: wistful look, wistful sigh, wistful memory
quote_en: She gave a wistful sigh as she watched the children play outside.
quote_zh: Boseman stared at the hills with a wistful expression, remembering his father who had passed through the Cherokee lands before the Trail of Tears—a nostalgia for a country that had already been stolen from its rightful owners.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "wistful" to describe Boseman's nostalgia, which is a form of mourning for a world that no longer exists—his wistfulness is not for the loss of the Cherokee (he does not mourn them) but for his own past, which is also lost.
ex_zh: The novel's wistful moments (Boseman's, Ethel's, the white settlers') are all tinged with irony—the wistfulness is for things that were stolen from others, and the novel asks us to feel the wistfulness but also to recognize its blindness, to see that the objects of wistful longing were never rightfully owned.

def_en: enthusiastic or passionate
def_zh: ardent supporter, ardent desire, ardent love
quote_en: She was an ardent supporter of women's suffrage throughout her life.
quote_zh: Lander was an ardent believer in the power of words to change the world, but his eloquence could not stop the bullets that cut him down on the Valentine farm—proof that passion alone is never enough against organized violence.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ardent" to describe Lander's passionate commitment to the cause, but the novel is ambivalent about ardor—ardor is inspiring, but it is also vulnerable, and the ardor of the idealist is no match for the cold violence of the mob.
ex_zh: The novel's ardent characters (Lander, Royal, the abolitionists) are all people who believe deeply in the possibility of change, but the novel shows that ardor without strategy is suicide—the Valentine farm is destroyed not because the residents lacked ardor but because they lacked the means to defend themselves against those who had more than ardor.

def_en: persuasive language; the art of effective speaking
def_zh: political rhetoric, empty rhetoric, rhetoric of freedom
quote_en: The candidate's rhetoric won over the crowd, but his promises proved empty.
quote_zh: The rhetoric of the Declaration of Independence—"all men are created equal"—was recited by slaves like Michael on the Randall plantation, each word a bitter mockery of the nation that refused to apply its own principles to them.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "rhetoric" to expose the gap between American ideals and American reality—the rhetoric of liberty is beautiful, but it is also a lie, and the enslaved know it is a lie because they live the reality that contradicts it.
ex_zh: The novel's rhetoric (the Declaration, the speeches of Lander, the words of the masters) is always suspect—words can be used to liberate, but they can also be used to deceive, and the novel asks us to listen to the rhetoric but also to look at the facts, to see the gap between what is said and what is done.

def_en: a bruise; an injury that does not break the skin
def_zh: multiple contusions, contusion of the arm, contusion and abrasion
quote_en: The doctor noted multiple contusions on the patient's arms and torso.
quote_zh: The contusions on Cora's body from Ridgeway's beatings were nothing compared to the internal scars left by watching Caesar dragged to the jail and Lovey disappear into the night—wounds that no poultice could heal.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "contusion" to name the visible injuries that the slave system inflicts, but the novel insists that the invisible injuries—the psychological wounds, the traumas, the losses—are more damaging and longer-lasting than any contusion.
ex_zh: The novel's contusions (the whip marks, the scars, the bruises) are the physical evidence of violence, but the novel's true subject is the damage that cannot be seen—the contusions heal, but the memory of the contusion does not, and the novel is an attempt to make the invisible scars visible.

def_en: great embarrassment or shame; the discipline of the body
def_zh: mortification and shame, public mortification, sense of mortification
quote_en: To his mortification, he realized he had been speaking with his fly unzipped.
quote_zh: Ethel's mortification at being exposed as a harbor of fugitives was swallowed by the greater terror of the hanging tree—a reminder that shame is a luxury reserved for those who are not fighting for their lives.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "mortification" to describe Ethel's shame, but the novel suggests that mortification is a privilege of the powerful—the enslaved have no time for mortification because they are too busy trying to survive, and the mortification of the white woman is a distraction from the real violence.
ex_zh: The novel's mortifications (Ethel's, the white townspeople's, the reader's) are all forms of discomfort that are minor compared to the suffering of the enslaved, and the novel asks us to question our own mortification—is it genuine empathy, or is it a way of making their suffering about us?

def_en: lacking in quantity or quality; sparse
def_zh: meagre portion, meagre income, meagre resources
quote_en: The refugees survived on a meagre ration of rice and beans each day.
quote_zh: The meagre possessions that fugitives carried—a handful of yams, a flint, a stolen pair of shoes—were worth more than gold, for they represented the difference between reaching the North and dying in the swamp.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "meagre" to describe the fugitives' supplies, but the novel shows that meagre resources can be enough if they are used wisely—the yams that Cora carries are meagre, but they keep her alive, and the meagre is precious because it is all they have.
ex_zh: The novel's meagre possessions (the yams, the flint, the stolen shoes) are the material basis of the fugitive's survival, and the novel insists that we value the meagre—not because it is valuable in itself, but because it is what the fugitive has, and the fugitive's life depends on it.

def_en: an adventurous or reckless incident
def_zh: youthful escapade, daring escapade, escapade and adventure
quote_en: The boys' weekend escapade involved camping in a forbidden forest.
quote_zh: Mabel's overnight escapade into the swamp was meant to be a brief taste of freedom before returning to her daughter, but a cottonmouth's bite transformed a small adventure into a death sentence—and left Cora to wonder for the rest of her life what had become of her mother.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "escapade" to describe Mabel's flight, but the word is ironic—what seems like an escapade is actually a matter of life and death, and the lightness of the word contrasts with the heaviness of the outcome.
ex_zh: The novel's escapades (Mabel's, Cora's, the underground railroad's) are all framed as adventures, but the novel strips away the romance to show the terror—an escapade is only an escapade if you survive, and the novel refuses to pretend that survival is guaranteed.

def_en: difficult to understand or interpret
def_zh: inscrutable expression, inscrutable mystery, inscrutable and opaque
quote_en: The detective faced an inscrutable smile from the suspect.
quote_zh: Homer's inscrutable expression, whether he was chaining a fugitive or recording Ridgeway's dictation, made him seem like a creature from another world—a black boy who had somehow made himself indispensable to the man who hunted his own kind.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "inscrutable" to describe Homer, whose motives and emotions are impossible to read—his inscrutability is a defense mechanism, a way of surviving in a world where showing your feelings can get you killed.
ex_zh: The novel's inscrutable characters (Homer, the Hob women, the survivors) all wear masks that are impossible to penetrate, and the novel suggests that inscrutability is not a character trait but a survival strategy—the enslaved learn to be inscrutable because the alternative is to be read and exploited.

def_en: to work with or use diligently; to travel a route regularly
def_zh: ply one's trade, ply the waters, ply between
quote_en: The boat plies the river between the two cities every morning.
quote_zh: The slave catcher's wagon plied the roads of the South, each mile a fresh opportunity for cruelty, but the underground railroad's tracks plied a different geography—one that existed in darkness, beneath the feet of masters who never knew what passed beneath them.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ply" to contrast two different geographies—the open roads of the slave catcher and the hidden tunnels of the underground railroad—both are plied regularly, but one is visible and one is secret, and the novel's geography is the secret one.
ex_zh: The novel's plying (of the underground railroad, of the slave catcher's routes, of the fugitive's path) is the action of the narrative—the novel plies back and forth between Georgia and Indiana, between the visible and the invisible, between the surface and the depths, and in doing so, it maps a territory that exists nowhere but in the imagination of the fugitive and the reader.

def_en: a structure for hanging criminals
def_zh: gallows and gibbet, gallows humor, sent to the gallows
quote_en: The condemned man was led to the gallows at dawn.
quote_zh: The gallows on the Randall plantation stood as a permanent warning, but for every slave who saw them and stayed, another looked at the same wood and decided that death by hanging was preferable to one more day in chains.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gallows" as a symbol of the slave system's ultimate threat—the gallows are meant to terrify, but the novel shows that terror can backfire, turning the threat of death into a promise of release.
ex_zh: The novel's gallows (the hanging tree in North Carolina, the gallows on the Randall plantation, the bodies on the Freedom Trail) are all meant to enforce submission, but they also create martyrs—the gallows cannot kill the idea of freedom, and the sight of a body hanging can inspire as many fugitives as it terrifies.

def_en: charming or enchanting, often deceptively
def_zh: beguiling smile, beguiling promise, beguiling and seductive
quote_en: The beguiling advertisement promised wealth but delivered only debt.
quote_zh: South Carolina's beguiling promise of colored uplift—good wages, education, housing—was a mask for the sterilization programs and medical experiments that treated black bodies as raw material for white science.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "beguiling" to describe the promises of South Carolina, which are designed to seduce the colored population into compliance—the beguiling surface hides a terrible reality, and the novel warns against being beguiled by the appearance of freedom.
ex_zh: The novel's beguiling surfaces (the whitewashed dormitories, the Griffin Building, the museum exhibits) are all attractive, but they are also deceptive—the novel teaches us to look beneath the beguiling surface, to see the violence that is hidden behind the beauty, and to resist the seduction of the beguiling.

def_en: the process of declining or worsening
def_zh: moral degeneration, physical degeneration, degeneration and decay
quote_en: The degeneration of the old building was accelerated by years of neglect.
quote_zh: Ridgeway saw Terrance Randall's degeneration from competent master to drunken sadist as a cautionary tale: the plantation system did not just corrupt the enslaved; it also rotted the souls of those who profited from it.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "degeneration" to describe the effect of slavery on the masters—the system does not only degrade the enslaved; it also degrades the enslavers, turning them into monsters, and the degeneration is inevitable because absolute power corrupts absolutely.
ex_zh: The novel's degenerations (Terrance's, the slave catchers', the entire slaveholding class) are the moral costs of the system—the novel shows that no one emerges from slavery unscathed, and the degeneration of the masters is as real as the suffering of the slaves, though it is less visible and less pitiable.

def_en: a woman who lives with a man but is not his wife, especially in societies where such arrangements are recognized
def_zh: royal concubine, concubine and consort, kept as a concubine
quote_en: The emperor kept several concubines in the forbidden palace.
quote_zh: Terrance Randall treated the enslaved women of his plantation as concubines to be taken at will, his power to violate without consequence the ultimate expression of the dehumanization at slavery's core.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "concubine" to name the sexual exploitation that is central to the slave system—the enslaved woman's body is not her own, and the master's right to use it as he wishes is a fundamental feature of slavery, not an aberration.
ex_zh: The novel's concubines (the women taken by Terrance, the women in the dormitories, the victims of the patrollers) are all denied the dignity of consent, and the word "concubine" exposes the sexual violence that is often hidden behind the euphemisms of the slave system—the concubine is a slave, and her status as a concubine does not protect her from being treated as property.

def_en: a young dog; an insolent youth
def_zh: wolf whelp, whelp and cub, insolent whelp
quote_en: The mother dog nursed her whelps in the warm corner of the barn.
quote_zh: Ridgeway dismissed Terrance as a whelp who had inherited his father's plantation but none of his competence—yet even a whelp with a whip was more than dangerous enough to destroy the lives of every slave under his control.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "whelp" as a term of contempt for Terrance, but the novel shows that even a contemptible whelp can do enormous damage when given absolute power—the whelp is not less dangerous because he is immature; he is more dangerous because he has no restraint.
ex_zh: The novel's whelps (Terrance, the young patrollers, the children of masters) are all products of the slave system, and their whelpishness does not excuse their cruelty—the novel insists that we hold them accountable, even if they are young, even if they are products of their environment, because the cruelty is real regardless of the age of the perpetrator.

def_en: a natural object or animal believed to have spiritual significance
def_zh: totem pole, animal totem, totem and symbol
quote_en: The tribe's totem was a wolf, representing loyalty and courage.
quote_zh: The burned oak tree in the park of the North Carolina town became a totem of terror, its branches decorated not with sacred symbols but with the bodies of those who had dared to believe that freedom was possible.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "totem" to describe the hanging tree, which has become a sacred object for the white community—it is a totem of white supremacy, a symbol of the violence that maintains the racial order, and it is worshiped as a god.
ex_zh: The novel's totems (the hanging tree, the whipping post, the auction block) are all objects of veneration for the slave system—they are the sacred symbols of a religion of violence, and the novel forces us to see them as totems, to recognize that the slave system has its own gods, and those gods demand human sacrifice.

def_en: affected by yellowing of the skin; cynical or prejudiced
def_zh: jaundiced view, jaundiced eye, jaundiced perspective
quote_en: The journalist had a jaundiced view of politics after decades of covering corruption.
quote_zh: Ridgeway's jaundiced eye saw every act of kindness as weakness and every slave's hope as a flaw to be corrected, but his cynicism could not extinguish the one thing he most despised: the stubborn, irrational, undying belief that freedom was worth dying for.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "jaundiced" to describe Ridgeway's worldview, which is colored by his hatred—his jaundiced eye sees only what it wants to see, and it cannot see the hope that drives the fugitives, because hope is invisible to a jaundiced eye.
ex_zh: The novel's jaundiced characters (Ridgeway, Terrance, the slave catchers) are all blind to the humanity of the enslaved, and their jaundiced vision is a form of moral disease—the novel shows that the jaundiced eye is not a sign of wisdom but a symptom of corruption, and that the cure for jaundice is to see clearly, to recognize the humanity that the jaundiced eye refuses to see.

def_en: a rude or ill-mannered person
def_zh: boor and lout, ignorant boor, drunken boor
quote_en: The boor interrupted every conversation with loud, ignorant comments.
quote_zh: The mayor was a corrupt boor, Ridgeway observed, but even a boor could recognize profit—and the slave catcher's business depended on the greed of men who would sell their own mothers for a few extra bales of cotton.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "boor" to describe the corrupt officials who enable the slave system—they are boors, but their boorishness does not prevent them from being effective agents of oppression, because the system does not require refinement, only greed and cruelty.
ex_zh: The novel's boors (the mayor, the judge, the patrollers) are all coarse, uneducated men, but they are also powerful—the novel shows that boorishness is not an obstacle to power but often its companion, and that the most refined gentlemen can be just as cruel as the most boorish louts when it comes to protecting the slave system.

def_en: childishly sulky or bad-tempered
def_zh: petulant child, petulant remark, petulant and whining
quote_en: The petulant child threw his toys when he didn't get his way.
quote_zh: Terrance's petulant cruelty—whipping a slave for a drop of wine on his cuff—revealed that his power was not strength but a child's tantrum armed with a cane, capable of destroying lives on a whim.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "petulant" to describe Terrance's cruelty, which is not the calculated cruelty of a strategic oppressor but the random cruelty of a spoiled child—his petulance makes him more dangerous, not less, because he is unpredictable, and the slaves can never know what will trigger his next tantrum.
ex_zh: The novel's petulant characters (Terrance, the young masters, the capricious slaveholders) are all evidence that the slave system is not a rational system but an arbitrary one—the petulance of the masters is a feature, not a bug, because the system is designed to give them absolute power, and absolute power produces petulance.

def_en: a period of isolation to prevent the spread of disease
def_zh: quarantine zone, quarantine period, quarantine and isolation
quote_en: The ship was placed under quarantine after several passengers showed signs of fever.
quote_zh: The town of yellow fever became a quarantine zone that even Ridgeway dared not enter, but the moral quarantine of the South—its isolation from the growing antislavery sentiment of the North—could not stop the underground railroad from slipping through the cracks.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "quarantine" to describe both the literal isolation of a disease-stricken town and the metaphorical isolation of the South from the rest of the world—the South tries to quarantine itself from abolitionist ideas, but the underground railroad is the disease that breaks the quarantine.
ex_zh: The novel's quarantines (the fever town, the slave states, the plantation) are all attempts to contain what cannot be contained—the desire for freedom is contagious, and no quarantine can stop it from spreading, because the underground railroad is the vector, and the fugitives are the carriers of a virus that will eventually infect the whole nation.

def_en: an act that violates a law or command
def_zh: moral transgression, transgression of boundaries, transgression and sin
quote_en: His transgression of the company's ethics policy resulted in immediate termination.
quote_zh: For a slave, the only true transgression was being born black in a white man's country; every attempt to escape, to learn, to love, to live was punished as a crime against the natural order of things.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "transgression" to name the supposed crime of being black in America—the system defines black existence itself as a transgression, and the enslaved are punished not for what they do but for who they are.
ex_zh: The novel's transgressions (reading, running, loving) are all acts that would be innocent in a free society, but in the slave system they are capital crimes—the novel shows that the concept of transgression is a tool of oppression, and that the only real transgression is to accept the system's definition of transgression without question.

def_en: a punishment or fate that someone deserves
def_zh: get one's comeuppance, final comeuppance, deserved comeuppance
quote_en: The arrogant CEO finally got his comeuppance when the company collapsed.
quote_zh: Ridgeway believed that runaways deserved their comeuppance, but in the end, it was he who lay broken at the bottom of a tunnel, whispering his American imperative to a black boy who wrote it all down—a comeuppance delivered by the very people he had hunted.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "comeuppance" to describe Ridgeway's fate, which is a form of poetic justice—the slave catcher becomes the victim, and his comeuppance is administered by the fugitive he could not catch, a reversal that the novel presents as both satisfying and tragic.
ex_zh: The novel's comeuppances (Ridgeway's, Terrance's, the slave system's) are all partial and incomplete—the novel does not offer easy justice, and the comeuppance that comes to some characters does not undo the suffering they caused, but it does offer a glimpse of a world where the hunters become the hunted and the fugitives finally have their day.

def_en: based on random choice or personal whim rather than reason
def_zh: arbitrary decision, arbitrary rules, arbitrary and capricious
quote_en: The teacher's grading seemed arbitrary, with no clear criteria.
quote_zh: Terrance Randall's arbitrary punishments—a beating for a smile, a day in the stocks for a sneeze—were designed not to correct behavior but to remind every slave that their lives depended on the unpredictable mercy of a man who had none.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "arbitrary" to describe the cruelty of the slave system, which is not governed by reason or justice but by the whims of the masters—the arbitrariness is the point, because it keeps the enslaved constantly off-balance, never knowing what will provoke violence.
ex_zh: The novel's arbitrary violence (Terrance's, the patrollers', the mob's) is the signature of the slave system—the system is not rational, it is arbitrary, and the arbitrariness is a form of terror that is more effective than any consistent rule because it creates a state of permanent anxiety, and the enslaved can never relax, never feel safe, because the next arbitrary punishment is always just around the corner.

def_en: refusing to be persuaded or changed
def_zh: adamant refusal, adamant stance, adamant and unyielding
quote_en: Despite the evidence, she remained adamant that her conclusion was correct.
quote_zh: Caesar was adamant that Cora must join his escape, and his certainty—unshakeable even when she refused—eventually convinced her to risk everything, proving that sometimes the most stubborn fool is also the one who sees the truth most clearly.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "adamant" to describe Caesar's determination, which is a form of stubbornness—his adamant refusal to take no for an answer is what saves Cora, and the novel suggests that adamant hope is a virtue, even when it looks like foolishness.
ex_zh: The novel's adamant characters (Caesar, Cora, the underground railroad agents) are all people who refuse to give up, even when the odds are against them—their adamant persistence is the engine of the narrative, and the novel insists that being adamant is not the same as being irrational, because in a world that tells you that freedom is impossible, the adamant refusal to accept that lie is the only rational response.

def_en: simple and unsophisticated; made at home
def_zh: homespun wisdom, homespun fabric, homespun philosophy
quote_en: The old man offered homespun wisdom about the value of hard work.
quote_zh: The homespun clothes of the slaves—rough negro cloth that chafed the skin—were a uniform of bondage, but the same hands that wore them also wove the coded quilts that guided fugitives north on the underground railroad.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "homespun" to describe the material culture of the enslaved—their clothes are homespun, but so are their codes, their quilts, their songs—the homespun is the medium of resistance, and the novel shows that the simplest, most homespun things can be the most powerful weapons.
ex_zh: The novel's homespun objects (the quilts, the songs, the stories) are all forms of folk knowledge that the masters overlook because they are homespun—the novel insists that we pay attention to the homespun, because the homespun is where the resistance lives, and the underground railroad is a homespun operation, built by ordinary people using the materials at hand.

def_en: a short, simple song
def_zh: sing a ditty, folk ditty, cheerful ditty
quote_en: The children sang a cheerful ditty as they walked to school.
quote_zh: Jasper's monotonous ditties drove Ridgeway to murder, but the hymns of the enslaved were not merely noise—they were coded maps, calls to resistance, and laments for the dead that the white men would never understand.
