ex_en: Whitehead uses "ditty" to describe the songs of the enslaved, which sound simple to white ears but carry complex meanings—the ditty is a form of code, a way of communicating in plain sight, and the novel shows that the ditty is a weapon of the weak.
ex_zh: The novel's ditties (Jasper's hymns, the work songs, the spirituals) are all examples of the oral tradition that sustained the enslaved—the ditty is not just entertainment; it is a form of resistance, a way of preserving memory, a means of organizing rebellion, and the novel pays tribute to the power of the simple ditty in a world that tried to silence it.

def_en: to bring under control or domination
def_zh: subjugate the population, subjugate and oppress, subjugate by force
quote_en: The empire sought to subjugate all neighboring territories.
quote_zh: The white men of North Carolina sought to subjugate not only the black population but the very idea of black existence, passing laws that erased the race entirely—a subjugation so complete that even the memory of the enslaved was to be extinguished.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "subjugate" to describe the project of North Carolina's race laws, which aim not just to control the black population but to erase them from history—subjugation is not enough; the state wants annihilation, and the novel shows that the logic of slavery leads inexorably to genocide.
ex_zh: The novel's subjugations (of the enslaved, of the Indians, of the poor whites) are all part of the same American project—the subjugation of anyone who is not a white landowner, and the novel shows that the methods of subjugation change (whips, laws, medical experiments, lynchings) but the goal remains the same: to maintain the hierarchy of race at any cost.

def_en: a short period of rest or relief
def_zh: brief respite, welcome respite, respite from suffering
quote_en: The cool breeze offered a brief respite from the summer heat.
quote_zh: Jockey's birthday provided the slaves of Randall plantation with a rare respite from the daily grind of the cotton rows, but even as they danced, the shadow of the overseer's whip waited at the edge of the circle.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "respite" to describe the brief moments of relief that the enslaved are allowed—the respite is always temporary, always conditional, always shadowed by the threat of violence, and the novel shows that respite is not freedom but a pause in the machinery of oppression.
ex_zh: The novel's respites (Jockey's birthday, the Saturday nights on Valentine farm, the moments between whippings) are all fragile—they are not safe havens but pauses in the violence, and the novel insists that we see the shadow behind the respite, to understand that the respite is an illusion, a brief dream of freedom that will always be shattered by the return of the whip.

def_en: rude or disrespectful behavior
def_zh: insolence and impertinence, act of insolence, intolerable insolence
quote_en: The manager would not tolerate any insolence from the new employees.
quote_zh: For a slave, insolence could be a look held too long, a question asked, a foot that failed to step aside quickly enough—each act of perceived insolence a capital crime in a system that demanded absolute submission.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "insolence" to name the supposed crime of asserting one's humanity in the presence of a master—the system defines any assertion of self as insolence, and the punishment for insolence is death, because the system cannot tolerate the idea that the slave is a person.
ex_zh: The novel's insolence (Cora's look, Caesar's question, the fugitive's flight) are all acts that the system calls insolent, but the novel redefines insolence as courage—the insolent slave is the one who refuses to submit, who looks the master in the eye, who asks the forbidden question, and the novel celebrates that insolence as the beginning of liberation.

def_en: the realization or fulfillment of a plan or project
def_zh: come to fruition, reach fruition, fruition of dreams
quote_en: After years of planning, her dream of opening a bakery came to fruition.
quote_zh: Caesar's plan for escape came to fruition not in the way he imagined—with both of them reaching freedom—but in Cora's survival, which became the fruition of every hope he had carried from Virginia to Georgia.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "fruition" to describe the outcome of Caesar's plan, which is not the happy ending he hoped for but a partial fulfillment—his hope bears fruit, but the fruit is bitter, and the novel shows that fruition is not always what we imagine, but it is still fruition, still the realization of something that was once only a dream.
ex_zh: The novel's fruitions (Caesar's plan, Cora's escape, the underground railroad's success) are all partial, incomplete, and tragic—the novel refuses easy happy endings, but it also refuses despair, because even partial fruition is better than no fruition, and the hope that Caesar planted in Cora bears fruit long after he is gone, and that fruit is the novel itself.

def_en: concise and exact use of words; shortness of time
def_zh: brevity and conciseness, brevity of life, for brevity's sake
quote_en: The brevity of his speech was appreciated by the tired audience.
quote_zh: The brevity of a slave's life on the harshest plantations was measured in years, but the brevity of a free moment—a dance at Jockey's birthday, a stolen kiss in the schoolhouse—could sustain a soul through decades of suffering.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "brevity" to contrast the shortness of life with the shortness of joy—both are brief, but the brevity of joy is precious because it is all the enslaved have, and the novel shows that brief moments of freedom can sustain a lifetime of bondage.
ex_zh: The novel's brevities (the dances, the kisses, the moments of respite) are all fleeting, but the novel insists that brevity is not a reason to despair—the brevity of joy makes it more intense, more valuable, and the fugitive who has tasted even a brief moment of freedom will never stop running, because that brief taste is enough to fuel a lifetime of hope.

def_en: sly or cunning intelligence
def_zh: guile and deceit, use guile, guile and trickery
quote_en: The fox used guile to escape the trap set by the hunter.
quote_zh: Cora survived not through brute strength but through guile—learning to read, to hide her thoughts, to strike only when necessary, to wait for the moment when the master's back was turned and the door to the underground railroad stood open.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "guile" to describe Cora's survival strategy, which is not about physical strength but about cunning, patience, and intelligence—guile is the weapon of the weak, and the novel shows that guile can be more effective than force when you are fighting a system that is much stronger than you are.
ex_zh: The novel's guile (Cora's, the underground railroad's, the fugitives') is the central theme of the narrative—the novel itself is an act of guile, using the tools of the oppressor (language, narrative, the novel form) to tell the story of the oppressed, and the novel's guile is its greatest strength, because it allows the story to be told in a way that the oppressor cannot suppress.

def_en: a person who intrudes or meddles where they are not welcome
def_zh: unwelcome interloper, interloper and intruder, interloper in the family
quote_en: The old dog growled at the interloper who dared to enter his yard.
quote_zh: Cora felt like an interloper in the schoolhouse, surrounded by children who had learned their letters while she had been dodging the overseer's whip—an interloper not because she did not belong, but because the system had denied her the right to belong.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "interloper" to describe Cora's feeling of not belonging, which is imposed by the slave system—the system makes her feel like an interloper in spaces that should be open to her, and the novel shows that the feeling of being an interloper is a form of violence, a way of keeping the enslaved in their place.
ex_zh: The novel's interlopers (Cora, the fugitives, the black students in white schools) are all people who are made to feel unwelcome in spaces where they have every right to be—the novel insists that the interloper is not the problem; the problem is the system that defines them as interlopers, and the novel's goal is to make the reader feel like an interloper, to unsettle them, to make them question their own right to be where they are.

def_en: causing annoyance or frustration
def_zh: vexing problem, vexing question, vexing and irritating
quote_en: The vexing problem took the entire team three days to solve.
quote_zh: The most vexing question for the fugitives was not how to escape the plantation but how to trust anyone again after so many betrayals.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "vexing" to describe the question of trust, which is a vexing problem for the fugitives—they cannot survive without help, but they cannot trust without risking betrayal, and the novel shows that this vexing dilemma is central to the experience of the enslaved.
ex_zh: The novel's vexing questions (whom to trust, where to go, when to run) have no easy answers—the vexing is the texture of the fugitive's life, and the novel does not resolve the vexing but instead dwells in it, showing that the vexing is not a problem to be solved but a condition to be endured.

def_en: to harass or pester persistently
def_zh: badger with questions, badger and nag, badger into action
quote_en: The reporters continued to badger the politician with uncomfortable questions.
quote_zh: Sybil would badger Cora into joining the quilting circle, insisting that the girl needed more than just survival—she needed something to make her feel human again.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "badger" to describe Sybil's persistence in getting Cora to participate in community life—the badgering is a form of love, a way of pulling Cora out of her isolation, and the novel shows that badgering can be a kindness as well as a nuisance.
ex_zh: The novel's badgering (Sybil's, the Hob women's, the underground railroad agents') is all about refusing to let the enslaved disappear into their own trauma—the badgering is an insistence on community, on connection, on the idea that survival is not enough, that we must also live, and that sometimes we need someone to badger us into living.

def_en: a person who acts as a link between parties
def_zh: financial intermediary, intermediary in negotiations, act as intermediary
quote_en: The diplomat served as an intermediary between the warring nations.
quote_zh: The underground railroad relied on a network of intermediaries—station agents, conductors, sympathizers—each one a fragile link in a chain that stretched from Georgia to Canada.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "intermediary" to name the essential role of those who connect fugitives to the next stage of their journey—the intermediaries are the hidden infrastructure of liberation, and the novel shows that without them, the fugitives would never make it north.
ex_zh: The novel's intermediaries (Fletcher, Sam, Lumbly, Royal) are all ordinary people who take extraordinary risks—they are the glue that holds the underground railroad together, and the novel pays tribute to their courage, even as it acknowledges their limitations, their fears, and their occasional failures.

def_en: a person who makes or sells women's hats
def_zh: milliner's shop, milliner and dressmaker, milliner's craft
quote_en: The milliner displayed her finest bonnets in the shop window.
quote_zh: After escaping to the North, Sybil worked for a milliner in Worcester, stitching ribbons onto hats for white ladies who would never have spoken to her on the street.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "milliner" to describe the kind of work that fugitive women did in the North—respectable, low-paid, invisible labor that kept them alive but did not lift them out of poverty, and the novel shows that freedom in the North is not the end of struggle.
ex_zh: The novel's milliners (Sybil, the women of the Valentine farm, the domestic workers) are all engaged in the work of survival—the milliner's shop is not a paradise, but it is not the plantation either, and the novel insists that we honor the milliner's labor, that we see the dignity in stitching ribbons onto hats for white ladies who will never say thank you.

def_en: abstinence from alcoholic drink; moderation
def_zh: temperance movement, temperance society, temperance and sobriety
quote_en: The temperance movement gained momentum in the nineteenth century.
quote_zh: On the Valentine farm, temperance was the rule except on Saturday nights, when those with a taste for spirits partook and had something to think about at the next morning's sermon.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "temperance" to describe the rule of the Valentine farm, which is not a prohibition but a moderation—the farm allows for celebration, but it also insists on discipline, and the novel shows that temperance is a form of self-governance, a way of taking control of one's own body and choices.
ex_zh: The novel's temperance (the Valentine farm's rules, the fugitives' self-restraint, the discipline of the underground railroad) is all about the rejection of the excesses of the slave system—the masters indulge in cruelty and luxury, while the fugitives practice temperance, and the novel suggests that temperance is a moral virtue that the slave system has destroyed in the masters but preserved in the enslaved.

def_en: a stand for holding a book or notes while speaking
def_zh: podium and lectern, stand at the lectern, lectern and microphone
quote_en: The professor adjusted the microphone on the lectern before beginning his lecture.
quote_zh: Lander gripped the lectern as he spoke, his words weaving a spell over the audience, but the wooden stand could not protect him from the bullets that would soon tear through the meeting house.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "lectern" to symbolize the power of oratory, but also its vulnerability—the lectern is a place of authority, but it is also a target, and the novel shows that words alone cannot stop bullets, that the lectern is not a fortress, and that the speaker is always exposed.
ex_zh: The novel's lecterns (Lander's, the abolitionists', the preachers') are all sites of resistance, but they are also sites of danger—the lectern is where the truth is spoken, and the slave system cannot tolerate the truth, so it sends bullets to silence the speaker, and the novel asks us to remember that the lectern is a sacred place, and that those who stand at it are martyrs as well as orators.

def_en: having a friendly, informal manner
def_zh: folksy charm, folksy humor, folksy and down-home
quote_en: The candidate's folksy charm appealed to rural voters.
quote_zh: Gloria worked hard on eliminating her plantation inflections, but when conversation took a folksy turn, she would slip back into the cadences of the indigo fields where she had been born.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "folksy" to describe the way Gloria speaks when she is not performing for white audiences—her folksy speech is authentic, a connection to her past, and the novel shows that folksy is not a mark of ignorance but a mark of identity, a way of holding onto who you are in a world that wants you to become someone else.
ex_zh: The novel's folksy moments (Gloria's slips, the slaves' songs, the stories told in the quarters) are all forms of cultural preservation—the folksy is the folk, the people, the community, and the novel insists that the folksy is not inferior to the sophisticated, that the cadences of the indigo fields are as beautiful as any poetry, and that the folksy is where the resistance lives.

def_en: fond of company; sociable
def_zh: gregarious personality, gregarious and outgoing, gregarious nature
quote_en: The gregarious host made sure every guest felt welcome.
quote_zh: Sam was gregarious by nature, his cheerfulness a kind of armor against the horrors he witnessed as a station agent on the underground railroad.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "gregarious" to describe Sam's personality, which is a survival mechanism—his gregariousness allows him to make connections, to gather information, to blend in, but it also hides the trauma he carries, and the novel shows that the gregarious are often the most wounded.
ex_zh: The novel's gregarious characters (Sam, the station agents, the conductors) are all people who use sociability as a tool—their gregariousness is not mere friendliness but a strategic adaptation to a world where being liked can save your life, and the novel shows that gregariousness is a form of intelligence, a way of reading people and situations, a skill as valuable as any other.

def_en: a person who takes advantage of opportunities without regard for principles
def_zh: political opportunist, opportunist and schemer, rank opportunist
quote_en: The politician was dismissed as an opportunist who would say anything to get elected.
quote_zh: Mingo was an opportunist who had purchased his family's freedom through careful calculation, but his desire to close the farm to runaways revealed that self-preservation could look very much like betrayal.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "opportunist" to describe Mingo, whose survival strategy is to align himself with power rather than with the fugitives—the opportunist is not necessarily evil, but the novel shows that opportunism is a betrayal of solidarity, and that the opportunist who saves himself may be condemning others to death.
ex_zh: The novel's opportunists (Mingo, the informers, the turncoats) are all people who choose self-preservation over community—the novel judges them harshly, but it also understands them, because in a slave society, everyone is an opportunist to some degree, and the line between survival and betrayal is not always clear.

def_en: a person holding a high rank or office
def_zh: foreign dignitary, visiting dignitary, dignitary and official
quote_en: Foreign dignitaries gathered at the embassy for the state dinner.
quote_zh: The dignitaries who attended Big Anthony's roasting—sipping spiced rum while a man burned alive—were not monsters but ordinary men whose humanity had been eroded by the daily practice of slavery.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dignitary" to describe the powerful men who watch the torture of Big Anthony—their dignity is a mask, and the novel shows that the dignitary is often more monstrous than the lout, because the dignitary's cruelty is cold, calculated, and respectable.
ex_zh: The novel's dignitaries (the judges, the planters, the politicians) are all complicit in the violence of the slave system—their dignity is built on the suffering of the enslaved, and the novel insists that we see the dignitary not as a figure of authority but as a figure of moral corruption, a man who sips spiced rum while another man burns alive.

def_en: the act of spreading information widely
def_zh: dissemination of knowledge, dissemination of information, dissemination and distribution
quote_en: The internet has accelerated the dissemination of news across the globe.
quote_zh: The dissemination of abolitionist literature was illegal in the South, but the underground railroad smuggled pamphlets and newspapers along the same routes that carried fugitives north.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dissemination" to describe the spread of ideas that threaten the slave system—the dissemination of abolitionist literature is a form of resistance, and the novel shows that words can travel as far as fugitives, and that the dissemination of ideas is as important as the movement of bodies.
ex_zh: The novel's dissemination (of pamphlets, of stories, of the truth about slavery) is the novel's own project—The Underground Railroad is itself an act of dissemination, a way of spreading the story of slavery to readers who might not otherwise know it, and the novel insists that dissemination is a moral obligation, that to know is to be responsible, and that the dissemination of the truth is the first step toward justice.

def_en: marked with spots or patches of color
def_zh: dappled sunlight, dappled shade, dappled and spotted
quote_en: The dappled sunlight filtered through the leaves of the old oak tree.
quote_zh: The dappled shadows of the swamp provided cover for the fugitives, but every patch of light might reveal them to the patrollers who hunted them with dogs and torches.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dappled" to describe the light in the swamp, which is both beautiful and dangerous—the dappled shadows hide the fugitives, but the dappled light can expose them, and the novel shows that the natural world is not a refuge but a battleground, where every patch of light is a potential threat.
ex_zh: The novel's dappled landscapes (the swamp, the forest, the burned fields) are all ambiguous spaces—they are not safe, but they are not the plantation either, and the dappled light is a metaphor for the fugitive's condition: always between shadow and light, never fully hidden, never fully revealed, always moving, always watching, always afraid.

def_en: to dismiss or make seem unimportant
def_zh: belittle achievements, belittle and demean, belittle the efforts
quote_en: The manager's habit of belittling his employees destroyed team morale.
quote_zh: The white men of North Carolina sought to belittle the humanity of the colored population, calling them savages and beasts, but the bodies swinging from the Freedom Trail told a different story of who the real savages were.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "belittle" to describe the rhetorical strategy of the slave system—by belittling the humanity of the enslaved, the masters justify their cruelty, but the novel shows that the belittling is a lie, and that the bodies on the Freedom Trail are evidence not of the savagery of the enslaved but of the savagery of the enslavers.
ex_zh: The novel's belittling (of the colored population, of their suffering, of their resistance) is the language of the oppressor—the novel refuses to belittle, insisting instead on the full humanity of the enslaved, on the magnitude of their suffering, on the courage of their resistance, and in doing so, it exposes the belittling as a form of violence, a way of making the unspeakable seem speakable, the unthinkable seem normal.

def_en: the student who delivers the farewell speech at graduation
def_zh: class valedictorian, valedictorian speech, valedictorian and salutatorian
quote_en: The valedictorian thanked her parents and teachers in her emotional speech.
quote_zh: Lander was elected valedictorian of his college after four years of being forced to live in a broom closet, his academic triumph a small revenge against the racism that had tried to define him.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "valedictorian" to describe Lander's achievement, which is a triumph over the racism that tried to confine him—the valedictorian is a symbol of excellence, but the novel shows that excellence is not enough to protect you from the violence of the slave system, and that Lander's valedictorian status does not save him from the bullet that kills him.
ex_zh: The novel's valedictorians (Lander, the educated slaves, the black scholars) are all people who have achieved excellence despite the system, but the novel is ambivalent about such achievements—they are worth celebrating, but they are not enough, and the valedictorian who speaks at graduation may be dead before the year is out, because the slave system does not care about your valedictorian status, only about the color of your skin.

def_en: to promote or make widely known
def_zh: promulgate laws, promulgate a doctrine, promulgate and publicize
quote_en: The organization worked to promulgate the benefits of renewable energy.
quote_zh: The slave owners promulgated the myth of the happy plantation slave, but the spirituals sung in the quarters told the true story of suffering and the desperate longing for freedom.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "promulgate" to describe the propaganda of the slave system—the masters promulgate a false image of slavery as benevolent, but the spirituals promulgate the truth, and the novel shows that the battle over narrative is as important as the battle over bodies.
ex_zh: The novel's promulgation (of the slave system's lies, of the fugitives' truths) is the novel's own work—The Underground Railroad promulgates the truth that the slave system tried to hide, and in doing so, it participates in the long struggle over narrative, the struggle to tell the story of slavery in a way that honors the enslaved rather than the enslavers.

def_en: a sculpted model or representation of a person
def_zh: burn in effigy, effigy of a leader, hang in effigy
quote_en: Protesters burned an effigy of the dictator in the town square.
quote_zh: Lander's pamphlets were burned in bonfires along with his effigy, but the ideas they contained—of equality, of justice, of a world without slavery—could not be reduced to ash.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "effigy" to describe the symbolic destruction of Lander—the mob burns his effigy, but the effigy is not the man, and the ideas survive the burning, and the novel shows that the destruction of the effigy is a futile gesture, because you cannot burn an idea.
ex_zh: The novel's effigies (the burned pamphlets, the hanging dummies, the coon show caricatures) are all attempts to control reality through representation—the slave system tries to reduce the enslaved to effigies, to caricatures, to symbols, but the novel insists that the enslaved are not effigies, they are people, and that the effigy is a lie, a way of avoiding the truth, a way of making the real seem unreal, and the novel's job is to burn the effigies and reveal the real.

def_en: having irregular patches of two colors, typically black and white
def_zh: piebald horse, piebald pattern, piebald and spotted
quote_en: The piebald horse stood out among the solid-colored herd.
quote_zh: The piebald horses that pulled the buggy through Indiana were a patchwork of black and white—like the nation itself, a country that claimed liberty while built on the labor of the enslaved.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "piebald" as a metaphor for America—a patchwork of black and white, of liberty and slavery, of freedom and bondage—the piebald nation is a contradiction, and the novel shows that the piebald cannot be resolved into a single color, that the black and white are forever intertwined, and that the nation must learn to live with its piebaldness.
ex_zh: The novel's piebald imagery (the horses, the landscape, the nation) is a visual representation of the central theme—America is piebald, and the attempt to make it all white is a form of violence, a denial of the piebald reality, and the novel insists that we see the piebald, that we accept the piebald, that we build a future on the piebald rather than on the lie of racial purity.

def_en: in a state of severe disrepair
def_zh: ramshackle building, ramshackle cottage, ramshackle and dilapidated
quote_en: The ramshackle cottage looked as if it would collapse in the next storm.
quote_zh: The ramshackle station beneath the abandoned house had not been used in years, its wooden planks rotting, its tunnel leading nowhere—a ghost of the underground railroad's failed hopes.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ramshackle" to describe the abandoned stations of the underground railroad—they are ramshackle, falling apart, but they are also sacred spaces, the sites of past resistance, and the novel shows that the ramshackle is not worthless, that the ruins of the underground railroad are as important as its successes.
ex_zh: The novel's ramshackle places (the abandoned stations, the rotting schoolhouse, the burned farm) are all sites of memory—they are ramshackle, but they are not empty, and the novel insists that we visit the ramshackle, that we remember what happened there, that we honor the ramshackle as a monument to the struggle, because the ramshackle is all that is left, and the ramshackle is enough.

def_en: unpleasantly damp and cold
def_zh: dank cellar, dank and dark, dank and musty
quote_en: The dank basement smelled of mold and decay.
quote_zh: The dank tunnels of the underground railroad were a second womb for the fugitives—cold, wet, and dark, but the only path to the light of freedom.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dank" to describe the conditions of the underground railroad—the tunnels are dank, uncomfortable, dangerous, but they are also necessary, and the novel shows that the dank is not a deterrent but a passage, that the fugitive must go through the dank to reach the light, and that the dank is a crucible, a place of transformation.
ex_zh: The novel's dank spaces (the tunnels, the cellars, the hiding places) are all places where the fugitive is buried alive—the dank is the condition of the underground, and the underground is the only way north, and the novel insists that we not romanticize the dank, that we see it for what it is: cold, wet, dark, and terrifying, but also the only path to freedom, and that the fugitive who endures the dank is a hero not because the dank is easy but because it is hard.

def_en: the quality of being certain to happen
def_zh: inevitability of death, inevitability of change, historical inevitability
quote_en: The inevitability of death is a theme explored in many works of literature.
quote_zh: The inevitability of the Civil War was written in every whipping post and auction block, but even as the nation tore itself apart, the underground railroad kept running, carrying the enslaved toward a future that had to come.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "inevitability" to describe the coming of the Civil War—the war is inevitable, the novel suggests, because the contradiction between slavery and freedom cannot be sustained forever, and the underground railroad is both a symptom of that contradiction and a force that makes the inevitable happen sooner.
ex_zh: The novel's inevitabilities (the war, the collapse of the slave system, the survival of the fugitives) are all presented as both inevitable and contingent—the novel shows that history is not predetermined, that the inevitability of the war does not make the suffering of the enslaved less real, and that the underground railroad is a human creation, not a historical necessity, and that the fugitives themselves, through their courage and cunning, make the inevitable happen.

def_en: a person trained to assist women in childbirth
def_zh: certified midwife, midwife and doula, midwife's hands
quote_en: The midwife arrived just as the first contractions began.
quote_zh: Royal's mother was a midwife in Connecticut, bringing new life into the world while her son fought to deliver the enslaved from the death grip of the plantation system.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "midwife" to describe both Royal's mother and Royal himself—she delivers babies, he delivers fugitives, and the novel shows that the work of liberation is a form of midwifery, helping to bring something new into the world, something that is struggling to be born, something that the slave system tries to kill in the womb.
ex_zh: The novel's midwives (Royal's mother, the underground railroad agents, the fugitives themselves) are all engaged in the work of delivering the future—the novel shows that liberation is not a single event but a process, a long, painful labor, and that the midwives of freedom are not always thanked, not always recognized, but they are essential, and without them, the future would never be born.

def_en: a person who travels and sells goods
def_zh: street peddler, peddler and hawker, traveling peddler
quote_en: The peddler carried his wares in a large wooden cart pulled by a donkey.
quote_zh: Valentine's father was a white peddler who passed through every few months, acknowledging his son only within the walls of their house—a reminder that even the love of a father could be stolen by the color line.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "peddler" to describe Valentine's father, a man who moves between worlds but cannot fully belong to either—the peddler is a liminal figure, and the novel shows that the color line divides families, that the peddler's visits are a reminder of the impossibility of love across the racial divide in a slave society.
ex_zh: The novel's peddlers (Valentine's father, the traveling salesmen, the itinerant workers) are all figures of mobility in a society that wants to keep people in their place—the peddler is a threat to the order because he moves, because he crosses boundaries, and the novel shows that the peddler is a precursor to the fugitive, that the ability to move is the beginning of freedom, and that the peddler who sells his wares is also selling the idea that movement is possible.

def_en: a lover, especially one who is not married to their partner
def_zh: secret paramour, paramour and mistress, paramour of the king
quote_en: The queen's paramour was banished from the court after the scandal.
quote_zh: The slave woman who became her master's paramour bought temporary safety at the cost of her children, who would be sold away as soon as they were old enough to bring a profit.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "paramour" to describe the relationship between master and slave, which is not a love affair but a form of exploitation—the paramour is not a lover but a victim, and the novel shows that the master's "affection" is a trap, that the paramour is not safe, that her children will be taken from her, and that the paramour's position is not a privilege but a prison.
ex_zh: The novel's paramours (the enslaved women taken by their masters, the concubines, the victims of sexual violence) are all denied the right to consent—the word "paramour" suggests choice, but the novel insists that there is no choice, that the paramour is a slave, and that the language of romance is a cover for the violence of rape, a way of making the enslaved complicit in their own violation.

def_en: a close friend or companion
def_zh: crony and sidekick, political crony, crony capitalism
quote_en: The mayor appointed his cronies to all the important city positions.
quote_zh: Mingo and his cronies in the back of the meeting house amened every word he spoke, their loyalty purchased by the promise of a future where they would be the ones deciding who was worthy of freedom.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "crony" to describe Mingo's supporters, who are loyal not to the cause but to their own advancement—the cronies are opportunists, and the novel shows that the crony network is a form of corruption, that it undermines solidarity, and that the crony who amens every word is not a friend but a follower, and that followers can become betrayers when the wind changes.
ex_zh: The novel's cronies (Mingo's allies, the informers, the turncoats) are all people who choose power over principle—the novel condemns them, but it also understands them, because in a slave society, the crony network is a survival strategy, a way of buying safety by aligning with the powerful, and the novel shows that the crony is not a monster but a coward, and that cowardice is a kind of evil.

def_en: a person who inspires or influences others; a celebrity
def_zh: literary luminary, luminary of the field, luminary and leader
quote_en: The conference attracted luminaries from the world of science and literature.
quote_zh: The Valentine farm hosted luminaries like Elijah Lander, whose speeches brought tears to the eyes of former slaves who had never heard a black man speak of freedom with such eloquence.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "luminary" to describe Lander, who is a star in the abolitionist world—the luminary is a source of light, but the novel shows that the luminary is also vulnerable, that the light of the luminary attracts bullets, and that the luminary who inspires others may not be able to save himself.
ex_zh: The novel's luminaries (Lander, the orators, the leaders) are all figures of hope—the novel celebrates them, but it also mourns them, because the luminary is a target, and the slave system destroys its brightest lights, and the novel asks us to remember that the luminary is not a god but a human being, that his light is borrowed, that it comes from the same source as the fugitive's torch, and that the luminary and the fugitive are the same: both trying to light the way out of darkness.

def_en: not able to be seen through; difficult to understand
def_zh: opaque glass, opaque language, opaque and obscure
quote_en: The professor's explanation was opaque, leaving the students more confused than before.
quote_zh: Lander was plainspoken but opaque; his speeches moved the heart but left the mind uncertain of what he actually proposed—a ambiguity that made him both inspiring and dangerous.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "opaque" to describe Lander's rhetoric, which is beautiful but unclear—the opacity is a strategy, a way of speaking truth without saying it directly, a way of evading the censor, and the novel shows that opacity can be a form of protection, a way of hiding in plain sight, a way of saying what cannot be said.
ex_zh: The novel's opaque moments (Lander's speeches, the coded messages, the quilts) are all examples of communication that is not transparent—the opacity is not a failure but a choice, a way of speaking to those who have ears to hear, a way of keeping secrets from the enemy, and the novel shows that in a slave society, opacity is a virtue, that the most dangerous truths are often the most opaque, and that the fugitive who learns to read the opaque is the one who survives.

def_en: an essential or urgent thing; a command
def_zh: moral imperative, categorical imperative, imperative to act
quote_en: It is an imperative that we address climate change before it is too late.
quote_zh: Ridgeway's American imperative—the belief that the white race was destined to conquer and enslave—was nothing more than a justification for theft and murder dressed up as destiny.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "imperative" to describe Ridgeway's ideology, which he presents as a moral duty but which the novel exposes as a lie—the imperative is a command, but the novel asks who gives the command, and who is commanded, and the answer is that the imperative is a self-serving fiction, a way of making violence seem necessary, a way of dressing up theft and murder as destiny.
ex_zh: The novel's imperatives (Ridgeway's, the slave system's, the nation's) are all challenged by the novel—the novel shows that the imperative to enslave is not a command from God or history but a choice, a choice made by human beings, and that the imperative can be refused, that the imperative to be free is stronger than the imperative to enslave, and that the fugitive who runs is obeying a different imperative, one that Ridgeway cannot understand and cannot stop.

def_en: an outline or shape; a line on a map joining points of equal height
def_zh: contour of the land, contour line, contour and silhouette
quote_en: The contour of the mountain was visible against the setting sun.
quote_zh: The contour of the land told the fugitives where to hide and where to run—the dips and rises of the earth, the curve of the river, the shape of the forest that might shelter them or betray them.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "contour" to describe the landscape that the fugitives must read—the contour is a map, a set of clues, a language that the enslaved must learn, and the novel shows that the contour is a text, that the fugitives are readers of the earth, that the contour of the land is as important as the contour of the sky, and that the fugitive who can read the contour can find the way north.
ex_zh: The novel's contours (the landscape, the tunnels, the bodies) are all surfaces that must be read—the contour of Cora's body is marked by the whip, the contour of the land is marked by the plantation, and the novel shows that the contour is a record, a testimony, a story written in scars and furrows, and that the fugitive who learns to read the contour can find the path to freedom, because the contour tells the truth that the masters try to hide.

def_en: excessively talkative, especially about trivial matters
def_zh: garrulous old man, garrulous and loquacious, garrulous chatter
quote_en: The garrulous old man cornered anyone who would listen to his stories.
quote_zh: Sam was garrulous by nature, his endless chatter a way of filling the silence that threatened to overwhelm him after the fire that destroyed his house and his friend Caesar.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "garrulous" to describe Sam's talkativeness, which is a defense against silence—the garrulous is not a flaw but a survival strategy, a way of keeping the darkness at bay, and the novel shows that the garrulous person is often the one who has seen too much, who cannot bear to be alone with his thoughts, and who uses words as a shield.
ex_zh: The novel's garrulous characters (Sam, the station agents, the storytellers) are all people who talk to stay alive—their garrulousness is a form of resistance, a refusal to be silent, a way of holding onto the community, and the novel shows that the garrulous are not annoying but heroic, that their endless chatter is a form of testimony, and that the garrulous voice is the voice of survival, the voice that says, "I am still here, I am still talking, and as long as I talk, I am not dead."

def_en: involving immoral or dishonorable actions; dirty
def_zh: sordid affair, sordid details, sordid and squalid
quote_en: The journalist uncovered the sordid details of the corruption scandal.
quote_zh: The sordid truth of South Carolina's colored uplift program was that the dormitories and schools were a mask for sterilization experiments, turning the dream of freedom into a nightmare of medical exploitation.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "sordid" to describe the reality behind the beautiful surface of South Carolina—the sordid is what is hidden, the dirty secret, the thing that the white people do not want to see, and the novel shows that the sordid is the truth, that the beautiful dormitories are built on sordid experiments, and that the sordid cannot be hidden forever.
ex_zh: The novel's sordid revelations (the sterilization program, the medical experiments, the lynchings) are all things that the slave system tries to keep hidden—the novel insists on exposing the sordid, on showing the dirt beneath the whitewash, on making the reader see what the characters try not to see, and in doing so, the novel itself becomes a sordid document, a record of the sordid truth that America would rather forget.

def_en: showing that something is unworthy of consideration
def_zh: dismissive gesture, dismissive tone, dismissive and contemptuous
quote_en: The manager was dismissive of the employees' concerns about safety.
quote_zh: Ridgeway was dismissive of Terrance Randall's ornate cruelties, but his own methods—the chains, the beatings, the murder of Jasper—were merely a different shade of the same darkness.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "dismissive" to describe Ridgeway's attitude toward Terrance—he dismisses Terrance's cruelties as excessive, but the novel shows that Ridgeway's dismissiveness is a form of hypocrisy, that his own cruelties are just as bad, and that the dismissive is a way of avoiding self-reflection, a way of saying "I am not like him" when in fact you are.
ex_zh: The novel's dismissive characters (Ridgeway, the masters, the white townspeople) are all people who refuse to see their own complicity—the dismissive is a defense mechanism, a way of maintaining moral superiority while committing atrocities, and the novel shows that the dismissive is a lie, that the person who dismisses the cruelty of others is often the most cruel of all, and that the only way out of the dismissive is to look in the mirror and see the darkness in yourself.

def_en: a strong or habitual liking for something
def_zh: penchant for danger, penchant for drama, penchant for violence
quote_en: She had a penchant for collecting antique silver spoons.
quote_zh: Ridgeway's penchant for violence and odd fixations made it hard to find men willing to ride with him, but his obsession with recapturing Mabel's daughter drove him across state lines and into the burning heart of Tennessee.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "penchant" to describe Ridgeway's character—his penchant for violence is not just a habit but a compulsion, and the novel shows that the penchant is a form of addiction, that Ridgeway cannot stop hunting because the hunt is who he is, and that his penchant will eventually destroy him.
ex_zh: The novel's penchants (Ridgeway's for violence, Terrance's for cruelty, the slave system's for exploitation) are all forms of obsession—the penchant is what drives the characters, what makes them who they are, and the novel shows that the penchant is both a strength and a weakness, that the penchant that makes Ridgeway a great slave catcher also makes him a failure, because he cannot let go, cannot see that the hunt has become his prison, and that the penchant that defines him will be his undoing.

def_en: an outcast or someone who is avoided
def_zh: social pariah, pariah and outcast, become a pariah
quote_en: After the scandal, he became a pariah in the community he once led.
quote_zh: Cora was a pariah even among the Hob women, her mother's abandonment marking her as cursed, but it was precisely her outcast status that gave her the courage to run when Caesar came with his plan.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "pariah" to describe Cora's status on the plantation—she is an outcast, avoided by others, but the novel shows that being a pariah is not a weakness but a strength, because the pariah has nothing to lose, and the pariah is free to run because no one will miss her, and the pariah's isolation is the condition of her liberation.
ex_zh: The novel's pariahs (Cora, the Hob women, the fugitives) are all people who have been pushed to the margins—the novel insists that the pariah is not to be pitied but celebrated, because the pariah sees the system from the outside, and the pariah's perspective is the clearest, and the pariah who is rejected by the community is the one who can save the community, because the pariah has no stake in the system, and the pariah's flight is the beginning of a new world.

def_en: a group of people with a shared characteristic; a companion
def_zh: cohort of students, same cohort, cohort and colleague
quote_en: The cohort of medical students graduated together after four years.
quote_zh: Ridgeway's cohort of slave catchers had dwindled to Boseman and Homer, the others driven away by his increasing unpredictability and the growing danger of a job that made enemies on both sides of the law.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "cohort" to describe the group of men who ride with Ridgeway—they are a cohort bound by a shared profession, but the novel shows that the cohort is unstable, that Ridgeway's violence drives them away, and that the cohort of slave catchers is a dying breed, a remnant of a system that is already beginning to crumble.
ex_zh: The novel's cohorts (Ridgeway's gang, the patrollers, the night riders) are all groups of men who enforce the slave system—the novel shows that the cohort is a fragile thing, that violence and betrayal are always present, and that the cohort that hunts fugitives is itself hunted by the law, by public opinion, by the changing times, and that the cohort of the oppressor is as vulnerable as the community of the oppressed.

def_en: showing malicious ill will
def_zh: spiteful remark, spiteful and vindictive, spiteful behavior
quote_en: The spiteful gossip spread rumors about her former friend.
quote_zh: Ethel was not a cruel woman by nature, but her treatment of Cora—the cold silence, the rare grudging meals—was spiteful in a way that revealed how easily fear could curdle into hatred.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "spiteful" to describe Ethel's treatment of Cora—the spite is not innate but produced by fear, and the novel shows that spite is a response to powerlessness, a way of asserting control when you have none, and that the spiteful person is not evil but pathetic, a small soul who lashes out at those even smaller than herself.
ex_zh: The novel's spiteful characters (Ethel, the white townspeople, the informers) are all people who are afraid—the spiteful is the language of fear, the way that fear expresses itself when it cannot find a larger target, and the novel shows that spite is a poison that destroys the one who feels it as much as the one it is directed at, and that the only cure for spite is courage, the courage to face the source of fear rather than to take it out on someone weaker.

def_en: a descriptive term or phrase; a derogatory word
def_zh: racial epithet, epithet and slur, hurled epithets
quote_en: The racial epithet was scrawled on the wall of the mosque.
quote_zh: Every slave had heard the epithet "nigger" countless times—a word designed to strip away humanity, to reduce a person to a thing, to make the whip and the auction block seem natural.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "epithet" to name the most powerful weapon of the slave system—the epithet is not just a word but a tool of dehumanization, a way of making violence acceptable, and the novel shows that the epithet is a lie, that the person called "nigger" is a human being, and that the epithet is a form of violence that the novel refuses to repeat.
ex_zh: The novel's epithets (the racial slurs, the dehumanizing labels, the names that the masters call the enslaved) are all present in the novel, but the novel refuses to use them casually—the epithet is a loaded word, a word that carries centuries of violence, and the novel shows that the epithet is not just a word but a wound, and that the novel's job is to heal the wound by telling the truth, by refusing the epithet, by calling the enslaved by their names, and by insisting that the epithet is a lie, a weapon, a tool of oppression that the novel will not wield.

def_en: to instigate or stir up trouble
def_zh: foment rebellion, foment discord, foment and incite
quote_en: The exiled leader was accused of trying to foment revolution from abroad.
quote_zh: Lander's speeches were said to foment rebellion among the slaves, but the truth was that the rebellion already existed in every heart that beat beneath the lash—he merely gave it words.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "foment" to describe the accusation against Lander—the masters say he foments rebellion, but the novel shows that the rebellion is already there, that the fomenting is not the cause but the expression, and that the word "foment" is a way of blaming the speaker for the listener's thoughts, a way of criminalizing the truth.
ex_zh: The novel's fomenting (Lander's speeches, the abolitionist pamphlets, the underground railroad) is all accused of stirring up trouble, but the novel insists that the trouble is already there, that the system is the trouble, and that the fomenting is not the disease but the diagnosis, not the cause but the cure, and that the only way to stop the fomenting is to remove the cause, to end the slavery that makes rebellion necessary, and that the fomenting will continue until the system is destroyed.

def_en: self-assertive or arrogant, used especially of a person of lower social status
def_zh: uppity servant, uppity and insolent, get uppity
quote_en: The servants were dismissed for being uppity after they requested better working conditions.
quote_zh: Any slave who looked a white man in the eye, who learned to read, who ran away, was labeled uppity—a word that meant nothing more than refusing to accept the place assigned to you at the bottom of the American order.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "uppity" to name the accusation against any slave who asserts their humanity—the word is a weapon, a way of putting the enslaved in their place, and the novel shows that "uppity" is a compliment, that the uppity slave is the one who refuses to submit, who knows their worth, who will not bow, and that the uppity are the heroes of the story.
ex_zh: The novel's uppity characters (Cora, Caesar, the fugitives) are all people who refuse to stay in their place—the novel celebrates their uppityness, shows that uppity is not a flaw but a virtue, that the uppity are the ones who change the world, and that the word "uppity" is a badge of honor, a sign that you have refused to accept the lie that you are less than human, and that the uppity will inherit the earth.

def_en: in a confused, rushed, or disorderly manner
def_zh: run pell-mell, flee pell-mell, pell-mell and helter-skelter
quote_en: The children ran pell-mell down the hill, laughing and shouting.
quote_zh: After the shooting started, the residents of the Valentine farm fled pell-mell into the darkness, parents losing children, children losing parents, each fugitive suddenly alone again in a world that had taught them that safety was always an illusion.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "pell-mell" to describe the chaos of the massacre—the flight is pell-mell, disorderly, desperate, and the novel shows that the pell-mell is the reality of violence, that there is no order in the face of terror, only the scramble to survive, and that the pell-mell is the truth that the neat narratives of history cannot capture.
ex_zh: The novel's pell-mell moments (the massacre, the escapes, the panics) are all scenes of chaos—the novel refuses to impose order on them, refuses to make them neat, refuses to pretend that there is a pattern, and the pell-mell is the texture of the fugitive's experience, the way that the world falls apart when the guns start firing, and the novel's job is to show the pell-mell, to make the reader feel the chaos, to understand that freedom is not a straight line but a pell-mell flight through darkness, and that the only way out is through.

def_en: a heavy iron block on which metal is hammered
def_zh: hammer and anvil, anvil and forge, blacksmith's anvil
quote_en: The blacksmith's hammer rang against the anvil from dawn until dusk.
quote_zh: Ridgeway's father had worked the anvil, shaping iron into chains and plows, but his son shaped human beings into fugitives and corpses—two sides of the same American imperative, hammer and anvil, master and slave.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "anvil" as a metaphor for the slave system—the anvil is the hard surface on which things are forged, and the novel shows that America was forged on the anvil of slavery, that the hammer of the master shaped the bodies of the enslaved, and that the anvil is stained with blood, that the nation was built on violence, and that the anvil still stands, still waiting for the next blow.
ex_zh: The novel's anvil (the forge, the hammer, the chain) is the central image of the American project—the anvil is where the nation was made, and the novel shows that the anvil is also where the nation can be unmade, that the hammer that forged the chains can also break them, and that the fugitive who picks up the hammer can strike the anvil and ring a new sound, a sound of liberation, a sound that the masters cannot ignore, and that the anvil is not only a tool of oppression but also a tool of resistance, a place where new things can be forged, new futures, new worlds, new freedoms.

def_en: a soft, moist mass applied to the body to relieve soreness or inflammation
def_zh: apply a poultice, poultice of herbs, poultice and bandage
quote_en: The herbalist prepared a poultice of crushed leaves to treat the wound.
quote_zh: The Hob women applied a poultice of roots and brine to Cora's whip-scarred back, their gentle hands a small mercy in a world that offered the enslaved nothing but suffering.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "poultice" to describe the care that the enslaved give to each other—the poultice is a small act of kindness, a moment of healing in a world of violence, and the novel shows that the poultice is a form of resistance, a way of saying that the body matters, that pain matters, that the suffering of the enslaved is not invisible, and that the poultice is a gift, a small mercy that keeps the spirit alive.
ex_zh: The novel's poultices (the Hob women's remedies, the care of the community, the small acts of kindness) are all moments of grace in a brutal world—the novel insists that the poultice is not a cure, that the wounds will not fully heal, but that the poultice is a sign of solidarity, a sign that the enslaved are not alone, that they care for each other, and that the poultice is a small act of rebellion, a refusal to let the system win, a refusal to let the masters destroy everything, and that the poultice, in its smallness, is as important as any escape.

def_en: to blow away chaff from grain; to reduce a large group
def_zh: winnow the wheat, winnow out the weak, winnow and sift
quote_en: The coach had to winnow the fifty applicants down to a team of twelve.
quote_zh: The early death of most slave children was a brutal winnowing, leaving only the strongest to survive the fields—a calculation as cold as the one that separated wheat from chaff.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "winnow" to describe the process by which the slave system selects for survival—the winnowing is brutal, efficient, and the novel shows that the winnowing is not natural but engineered, that the system is designed to kill the weak, to leave only the strong, and that the winnowing is a form of eugenics, a way of breeding a population of the hardiest workers, a calculation that treats human beings as crops.
ex_zh: The novel's winnowing (the deaths of children, the selection of the strong, the discard of the weak) is the hidden logic of the plantation—the novel exposes the winnowing, shows that it is not an accident but a design, that the system winnows the enslaved to produce the most labor, and that the winnowing is a crime, a crime against humanity, a crime that the novel refuses to forget, a crime that the novel insists we see, and that the winnowing is not the end, that the winnowed survive, that the winnowed are the ones who run, and that the winnowing cannot winnow out the desire for freedom.

def_en: bad-tempered or combative; stubborn
def_zh: ornery old man, ornery and cantankerous, ornery disposition
quote_en: The ornery old mule refused to move no matter how much the farmer tugged.
quote_zh: The snake was ornery but harmless, or so Mabel told herself before the cottonmouth's fangs sank into her thigh—a reminder that even the most ordinary creature could carry death in its bite.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "ornery" to describe the snake that kills Mabel—the snake is ornery, stubborn, but the novel shows that the ornery is not a joke, that the ornery can kill you, that the ornery is the unexpected danger, the thing you thought was harmless, the thing that ends your life.
ex_zh: The novel's ornery characters (the snake, the patrollers, the small cruelties) are all things that seem minor but are deadly—the novel insists that the ornery is not to be underestimated, that the ornery is the texture of the slave system, the small, daily, stubborn cruelties that wear you down, that kill you slowly, that make life unbearable, and that the ornery is the enemy, the enemy that is always there, always ornery, always ready to bite, and that the fugitive who survives is the one who learns to see the ornery, to avoid the ornery, to run from the ornery, and to keep running until the ornery is behind them, and the only thing ahead is the open road, the north star, and the possibility of freedom.

def_en: a disorderly retreat; to defeat decisively
def_zh: put to rout, complete rout, rout the enemy
quote_en: The army's rout began when the general fled the battlefield.
quote_zh: The white posse's rout of the Valentine farm left bodies scattered across the meeting house floor, but the survivors who fled into the night would carry the memory of that massacre like a torch—one day to be lit again.
ex_en: Whitehead uses "rout" to describe the destruction of the Valentine farm—the rout is a defeat, a massacre, but the novel shows that the rout is not the end, that the survivors carry the memory, that the rout sows the seeds of future resistance, and that the routed are not defeated, not really, because they survive, and survival is a form of victory, a refusal to die, a refusal to let the rout be the last word.
ex_zh: The novel's routs (the Valentine farm, the underground railroad stations, the hopes of the fugitives) are all moments of destruction—the novel does not shy away from the rout, does not pretend that the good guys always win, shows that the slave system is powerful, that it can rout the resistance, that it can kill, that it can burn, that it can destroy. But the novel also shows that the rout is not the end, that the routed survivors will rebuild, that the routed memory will become a torch, that the routed hope will rise again, and that the rout is not the final chapter, that the final chapter is still being written, by the fugitives, by the survivors, by the readers, by the novel itself, and that the rout will one day be avenged, and the routed will become the routers, and the wheel will turn, and the slave system will fall, and the underground railroad will have the last laugh, because the underground railroad is not a thing but an idea, and ideas cannot be routed, cannot be killed, cannot be burned, cannot be stopped, and the idea of freedom will outlast any rout, any massacre, any posse, any slave catcher, any master, and the idea will keep running, keep moving, keep hoping, keep fighting, until the last chain is broken, the last whip is burned, the last slave is free, and the underground railroad is no longer needed, because the railroad will have reached its final station, and the name of that station is freedom.
